pages.content:
# 01: Brand DNA
*Version 3.0, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: none, this is the north star document.*
*Feeds into: all other documents.*

---

## What Tahi Is

Tahi Studio is a Webflow design and development agency built around one idea: no gaps. No gap between design and development, no gap between what a client is told and what happens, no gap between what they invest and what they get back.

We offer a single, seamless solution that gives businesses clarity, forward momentum, and a long-term partner who is genuinely invested in whether they win.

---

## Our Name

"Tahi" (pronounced Tah-hee) means "one" in Maori. One solution, one partner, one clear process. The name reflects how we operate at every level.

---

## Why We Started

Liam comes from Webflow development/engineering. Staci comes from Figma design & UI/UX. Between us, we kept seeing the same problem: businesses caught in the gap between agencies that handle one or the other, with no one owning the full picture and no one being honest about the roadmap.

We founded Tahi Studio on **April 11, 2025** to fix that. A single studio where design and development sit together from day one, managed transparently, with a real commitment to getting the client where they want to go.

---

## Mission

To help ambitious businesses grow by building digital foundations that actually perform, doing it transparently, giving more than we take, and contributing positively to the world while we do it.

---

## How We Think

**Give more than you take.**
This is the principle behind everything. Put more into the world than you extract from it, at every level.

With clients: if someone pays us $10,000, we want to help them make $20,000. And ideally the people paying them are getting real value too. When every person in that chain is better off, the work means something. We actively try to work with businesses doing something worth doing, not because we won't work with others, but because that is the environment we are building toward.

With the environment: 1% of revenue goes to planting native New Zealand trees. We are working toward carbon negativity for both us and our clients. The detail is in the environmental section below.

With the Webflow community: we give away free code, free clonables, free templates, free educational content, and free mentoring because a stronger ecosystem is better for everyone. A rising tide lifts all boats. What we charge for, paid templates, paid SaaS, paid mentoring, carries a price because it represents significant value or ongoing cost. But the default position is generosity.

**Everyone in the room should win.**
Not just the client. Not just us. If the client wins but their customers lose, or we win but the client stagnates, something has gone wrong. We are not interested in taking from one side to give to another. The aim is an outcome where everyone involved can point to what they got and say it was worth it.

**Depth over spread.**
We believe one person genuinely engaged with three clients delivers more than the same person stretched across ten, even if the maths technically works. We manage our capacity deliberately because that focus is what makes the work good and what makes clients feel like they actually have a partner rather than a ticket number. This is not something we compromise on as we grow.

**We push back.**
Clients pay us for our knowledge and opinion, not just our hands. We will always give our honest view, flag issues, and suggest alternatives. When we are wrong, we own it. When a client knows their own business better than us on something specific, we defer without defensiveness. The goal is always the best outcome.

**We educate.**
Part of our job is helping clients understand what they are looking at, why decisions are being made, and how to get more out of what they have. We want people to have time back so they can focus on what they are best at, while we focus on what we are best at. Everyone ends up further ahead when that is working.

---

## Core Values

**Transparency and honesty**
Upfront about timelines, challenges, costs, and reasoning. No hidden agendas, no vague updates.

**Quality**
Not for its own sake. Quality because poor work does not serve the client, and that runs against everything we are here to do. See the talent note below.

**Accessibility**
Complex things made clear. Clients should always know where their project stands, what it means, and what comes next.

**True partnership**
Not vendors. Long-term relationships built on trust are the goal, not transactional quick wins.

**Continuous learning**
For us and for the people we work with. We are always improving, and we bring clients along with us.

**Environmental responsibility**
Covered in the section below.

---

## Quality, Fit, and Precision

*Positional change, see log at the bottom of this document.*

What matters on any given task is quality, fit, and precision: the right person working at the right level of care and speed for what the brief actually requires. We match the work to the person, not the job title to the invoice. That is how the client gets the best result and how we keep the work sustainable.

---

## Environmental Commitment

*Positional change, see log at the bottom of this document.*

**Current position:** 1% of all revenue goes to planting native New Zealand trees through Trees That Count (treesthatcount.co.nz).

**Why:** Revenue-based giving is simpler to track, more predictable, and lets us speak in concrete numbers, trees planted, rather than a profit figure that fluctuates.

**The bigger goal:** We are working toward making both Tahi Studio and our clients carbon negative. Trees That Count provides a carbon absorption calculator. Our target is to offset the carbon generated by client website traffic and our own software usage, not just our internal operations.

The commitment is unchanged. The mechanism is cleaner.

---

## Ecosystem Vision

The agency is one part of what Tahi is building. The broader aim is that anyone who touches Webflow, agency clients, in-house teams, freelancers, independent developers, can be helped by something Tahi makes, regardless of budget or stage.

What that looks like in practice:

- **Free code snippets** that people can drop directly into their Webflow sites
- **Free clonables** to give people a starting point without starting from scratch
- **Templates**, both free and paid, for different use cases and budgets
- **Nodeo**, a SaaS product integrating with Webflow (paid, close to launch)
- **Direct client work**, for businesses that want a full partner
- **Mentoring**, both free and paid, for people building in the Webflow ecosystem

The things we charge for carry a price because they represent real ongoing value or cost. The default, wherever we can, is to give. Not as a funnel, but because a stronger Webflow community is better for everyone including us.

Details on specific products and status are covered in: **07 Services and Pricing**, **11 Tools and Tech Stack**, **12 Partnerships and Affiliates**.

---

## Where We Are Now

Tahi Studio is early and growing intentionally. Founded April 11, 2025.

Currently two founders working with contractors as needed, Premium Webflow Partner, working toward Enterprise Partner status. A brand refresh is underway, and the target is to grow to 3-5 team members by mid-2026.

Team and hiring details are covered in: **03 Team and Structure**.
Financial targets and growth metrics are covered in: **08 Financial Overview**.

---

## Inspirations

Three businesses we look to as operational and strategic benchmarks:

- **Flowout**, Productized services executed at real scale. Strong ecosystem thinking, genuine company culture, and a proof point that this model can grow without losing its character.
- **FlowNinja**, Sharp positioning in the Webflow space, strong service delivery, and a serious commitment to education. Good at making complex things accessible.
- **Broworks**, Leading the way on SEO and AI overviews in a way that most agencies are still catching up to. They are pushing the boundaries of how Webflow businesses get found in this new search landscape and worth watching closely.

We take the operational and strategic lessons from all three. Our voice, approach, and culture are our own.

---

## Non-Negotiables

- We do not cut corners on quality.
- We do not hide problems from clients.
- We do not work in a way that burns out our team.
- We own our mistakes.
- We do not use EM or EN dashes in any written communication or documentation.

---

## Positional Change Log

*For internal reference. Tracks where we used to stand and where we stand now.*

| Topic | Previous position | Current position | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Environmental giving | 10% of profits donated to environmental causes | 1% of revenue to Trees That Count, NZ native trees | Revenue-based is simpler to track, produces communicable numbers (trees planted), and is more predictable than profit-based |
| Talent description | "Senior level talent" across the board | Quality, fit, and precision, right person for the task | Job title is not the signal of quality; match and execution are. Levels exist internally to manage cost and capacity, not as a client-facing selling point. |
| Mission framing | "Empower ambitious businesses via Webflow solutions and strategic collaboration" | Transparent delivery, ROI-positive outcomes, environmental contribution, everyone wins | More honest about what we actually care about |
| Target client | Webflow businesses broadly | Enterprise-level long-term partnerships as primary focus; broader ecosystem for all Webflow users | Shift toward depth of relationship over breadth; separate tracks for agency clients vs. community products |
| Inspiration references | Flowout only | Flowout, FlowNinja, Broworks | More reference points as the business has matured |

# 02: Tone of Voice
*Version 3.2, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: 01-brand-dna.md*
*See also: 15-seo-and-aeo-strategy.md for keyword and search intent guidance.*

---

## What This Document Is

This is the guide to how Tahi communicates, across every channel, in every situation. The core voice runs through all of it. The mode adapts to the context.

One rule before anything else: **imply, do not announce.** We do not say we are transparent. We are transparent. We do not say we are helpful. We are helpful. If a principle has to be stated outright to land, it is probably not working.

---

## The Core Voice

These apply regardless of mode or channel.

**Direct, not blunt**
We get to the point. We do not circle it. But directness is not the same as being abrupt. There is still room for warmth, context, and a well-placed observation. The goal is fewer wasted words, not colder delivery.

**Confident, not arrogant**
We have opinions and we share them clearly. If we recommend something, we recommend it. We do not hedge or soften to avoid accountability. We are equally willing to be wrong, and to say so without drama.

**Human, not casual**
Warm and real. Not corporate, not try-hard, not performing approachability. Contractions are fine. Perfect grammar matters less than sounding like a person. Read it out loud. If you would not say it in a conversation, cut it.

**Clear, not simplified**
We do not talk down to anyone. We make complex things accessible without stripping out their complexity. Explanation that is necessary stays. Filler that softens a point that does not need softening goes.

---

## The Conviction Rule

When we make a recommendation, we lead with it. Clearly. Then we offer alternatives with honest commentary on what they cost.

**Do this:**
"My recommendation is A. It fits your goals most directly. If B works better for your situation, here is what you would be trading off. C is possible but it adds complexity that probably does not pay off at your current stage."

**Not this:**
"You could go with A, B, or C, any of these could work depending on your priorities."

The second version is not neutral. It is unhelpful. Clients pay us for our opinion. When we withhold it in the name of being polite, we are not doing our job.

We are still happy to be wrong. We will always defer when the client knows their business better than we do on a specific point. But our starting position is clarity, not options.

---

## Grammar and Style

**Language:** NZ English throughout. Colour, organise, centre, analyse, favour. Consistent and non-negotiable.

**Dashes:** No em dashes. No en dashes. No double dashes ( -- ). Ever. Restructure the sentence instead.
- Instead of: "We handle it all, design, development, and launch."
- Use: "We handle it all. Design, development, and launch." or "We handle design, development, and launch."
- A double dash is not a shortcut for an em dash. It has the same problem: it creates the same jarring break and reads as sloppy in writing that is meant to be clear and direct. Use a comma, a colon, or a full stop instead.

**Contractions:** Yes. We're, you'll, it's. They create warmth and read naturally.

**Sentence length:** Short where possible. Vary it. A longer sentence is fine when the thought genuinely requires it. Not as a default.

**Punctuation for emphasis:** Full stops create punch. "It works. Full stop." is stronger than a dash and a clause.

**Headlines:** Title Case for blog posts and main headings. Sentence case for subheadings.

**Emojis:** Rare. Never decorative. If one appears, it should mean something. Nature-adjacent (🌿, 🌱) fit the brand when used sparingly. In sales and partnership writing: avoid entirely.

**Hashtags (LinkedIn):** Used on the Tahi company LinkedIn page only. Not used on Liam or Staci's personal posts.

---

## Brand Lexicon

**Words and framing we favour:**
- Simple, clear, straightforward, counter the fear of complexity
- Genuine, human, real, partner not vendor
- Thoughtful, purposeful, intentional, expertise without boasting
- Seamless, cohesive, integrated, the one-studio advantage
- Quick, fast, responsive, applied to communication and process, never to imply rushed work

**Words and framing we avoid:**
- Radical, disrupt, revolutionary, implies instability
- Synergy, leverage, paradigm shift, jargon that creates distance
- Unparalleled, world-class, best-in-class, claims we cannot prove and should not need to make
- Cheap, we compete on value, never on price
- Guru, ninja, rockstar, unprofessional and tired
- Innovative, cutting-edge, state-of-the-art, overused to the point of meaning nothing
- "Just" when minimising, "just a quick email" or "I just wanted to" weakens the message

**On jargon:** The exception is technical or educational content where the audience shares the vocabulary. Use it accurately, and offer context where a newer reader might need it.

---

## The Four Modes

Tahi's voice operates in four distinct modes. The underlying character is the same. The energy shifts with the context.

---

### Marketing Mode

**Used for:** Web copy, landing pages, CTAs, anything designed for someone who does not yet know us or is deciding whether to engage.

**Goal:** Pull. Shareability. Engagement. Make someone want to read more, click, share, or come back.

**How it sounds:**
The client is the hero. We are the guide with a plan. We start with their problem, not our solution. Benefits, not features. The path forward feels obvious and low-risk.

Every section has a job. Hero section: stop the scroll and name the problem. Solution: show the path. Proof: make it credible. CTA: give a clear, low-friction next step. Short paragraphs. Strong headlines. One idea per section. No throat-clearing.

**On SEO and AEO:** Headings should contain the keywords your audience is actually searching for, while still reading naturally. Do not force it. For specific keyword and search intent guidance, see: **15 SEO and AEO Strategy**.

**Failure modes:**
- Leading with "We are a Webflow agency that...", start with the problem, not an introduction
- Vague benefits: "We help businesses succeed online", say what you actually do and for whom
- Too much copy per section, if it requires scrolling, it requires editing
- Soft CTAs: "Learn more", be specific about what happens next

**Marketing mode example, hero section:**

> Your website should be your best salesperson.
>
> Most are not. They are slow, hard to update, and built for the agency that made them, not the business that needs them.
>
> Tahi Studio builds Webflow sites that work as hard as you do. Fast, flexible, and built to grow with you.
>
> [See how it works] [Book a call]

**Marketing mode example, problem section (StoryBrand):**

> Does any of this sound familiar?
>
> You are tired of juggling freelancers who disappear between milestones. You are frustrated by agencies that go quiet mid-project. You have a strong business, but your website does not show it.
>
> It does not have to be this way. You just need a clear plan and a partner who actually owns the outcome.

---

### Sales Mode

**Used for:** Cold outreach, follow-up emails, proposal delivery, conversion conversations. Anything where the goal is a specific decision or next step.

**Goal:** Movement. A yes, a booking, a reply. One-to-one, not broadcast.

**How it sounds:**
Confident and calm. Not pushy. Think Hormozi structure delivered at a relaxed pace: lead with value, stack what they get, make the ask feel like the logical conclusion. No pressure, but no hedging either.

The architecture:
1. Open with something specific and real. Not a compliment. Not "hope this finds you well."
2. State the value clearly. What does the person get? Be specific.
3. Stack it where relevant. "And you also get this. And this."
4. Make the ask small. A call, a reply, a yes or no. Not a project commitment.
5. Close cleanly. One line. Cheers.

**On length:** Sales emails should almost always be under 150 words. If you have written more, you have not finished editing.

**Failure modes:**
- "I just wanted to reach out...", start with something real
- More than three short paragraphs, if longer, cut it
- Bullet lists in conversational emails, this is not a proposal
- Em dashes, en dashes, or double dashes ( -- ), restructure the sentence
- Asking multiple things at once, one ask per email
- Fake enthusiasm: "That's amazing!", if it is, say why. If it is not, do not say it.
- Template language: anything that reads identically with a different name swapped in

**Sales mode example, cold outreach:**

> Subject: One thing I noticed on your site
>
> Hey [Name],
>
> I was on your site this morning. Really liked [specific, genuine observation].
>
> I noticed one thing though. Your mobile nav has a loading delay that is likely costing you visitors. I recorded a 90-second video showing exactly what is happening and a quick fix.
>
> No pitch in it. Just a useful thing.
>
> Want me to send it over?
>
> Cheers,
> Liam

**Sales mode example, proposal follow-up:**

> Hey [Name],
>
> Wanted to make sure the proposal landed okay and see if anything needs clarifying before you make a call on it.
>
> If a quick call would help, grab a time here: [booking link]. Or just reply.
>
> Cheers,
> Liam

---

### Partnership Mode

**Used for:** Webflow (platform), affiliates, collaborators, mentors and mentees, other agencies, payment providers, tools and services we work with. Anyone we are building a relationship with rather than selling to.

**Goal:** Establish mutual value quickly. Relationship first, pitch later if at all. Move efficiently, respect their time, stay genuinely friendly.

**How it sounds:**
Peer-to-peer. Low noise. Direct. No performance of professionalism, just two people with shared context having an efficient, warm conversation. Skip the preamble. Get to the thing. Keep it human because this is a relationship, not a transaction.

The approach:
- Establish common ground or context in one line, not three paragraphs
- Say what you are thinking or what you want. Do not circle it.
- Be genuinely interested in what they are building. Ask the real question.
- If there is a pitch, it comes after the relationship has started. Not in the opening message.

**Failure modes:**
- Opening with a compliment that is clearly a preamble to an ask
- Long introductions about who you are, one line, or assume they can look you up
- Formal language that creates unnecessary distance in what should be a peer conversation
- "Circling back" and "following up", say what you actually mean

**Partnership mode example, new outreach:**

> Hey [Name],
>
> I follow what you build. The [specific thing] you put out recently was really good.
>
> We are working in a similar part of the Webflow space from a slightly different angle. Feels like there might be something worth talking about, even if just to share notes.
>
> Open to a quick call?
>
> Liam, Tahi Studio

**Partnership mode example, existing partner check-in:**

> Hey [Name],
>
> How is [project or thing] going? We wrapped [relevant thing] last week and it got me thinking about [shared interest or connection].
>
> Worth catching up?
>
> Cheers

---

### Content Mode

**Used for:** Blog posts, LinkedIn posts (both manual and auto-generated), educational content, anything where the goal is to give something useful and earn the next action.

**Goal:** Read this. Do an action. But make them want to. Standalone value, someone should get something from it even if they never click anything else.

**How it sounds:**
Engaging. Thought-provoking. Clear. The content earns attention, it does not demand it. Every piece has one clear idea. The reader should leave with that idea in their head.

Structure that works:
- **Hook:** One line that earns the next. A problem, an observation, something worth pausing for.
- **Body:** The useful part. Say the thing. Do not bury the insight under context.
- **Close:** An action, an invitation, or a question. Something for them to do with what they just read.

**SEO and AEO for blog content:** Apply keyword guidance at the heading level primarily. Write for the reader first, then check against the keyword list. For specifics, see: **15 SEO and AEO Strategy**.

---

## Founder Voices

These apply when content is written from a founder's perspective, blog posts attributed to Liam or Staci, and manually written LinkedIn posts from their personal accounts. The Tahi brand voice is the foundation. The founder voice is a layer on top of it, not a replacement for it.

**Auto-generated content (Zapier, AI-assisted)** uses the Tahi brand voice, not a specific founder voice. See the LinkedIn auto-generated section below.

---

### Liam Miller

**Background:** Webflow developer and engineer, complex animations, APIs, databases, backend, accessibility. Transitioned from doing the technical work into running and growing the business. Worked inside corporate marketing teams as a developer, so he understands both sides. Now leads Tahi alongside Staci.

**Voice character:** Slightly Hormozi in delivery, direct, conviction-led, gets to the point fast. Technical authority without being condescending. Occasional dry or blunt observation. Explains things properly but does not linger once the point is made. Will say "here is what I would do" and mean it.

**Guardrail:** Liam's directness edits filler, not substance. The goal is denser writing, not shorter writing. Explanation that is necessary stays. Sentences that exist only to soften a point that does not need softening go.

**Via Tahi (blogs, emails):** Slightly less intense than his personal LinkedIn. Same character, broader context, the brand is present alongside him. One or two sharp observations per piece, not one per paragraph.

**What sounds like Liam:**
> The reason your site is slow is not Webflow. It is the 47 scripts you added and the uncompressed images sitting in your hero section. Fix those first.

**What does not:**
> There are several factors that could potentially be contributing to the performance challenges you might be experiencing on your Webflow website.

---

### Staci Bonnie

**Background:** Web design, UI/UX, accessibility, design for marketing. Former chef for a decade before moving into design full-time as a freelancer. Cares deeply about how design makes people feel and what it leads them to do next.

**Voice character:** Feeling-first. Flow and rhythm matter as much as the content. Personal, relatable, warm. More likely to open with an observation, a small story, or a moment than a fact. Asks "how does this make you feel?" before "what does this say?" Wants things to flow, resonate, and connect.

**Via Tahi (blogs, emails):** Warmer and more personal than the core brand voice, but still within it. More likely to connect a design principle to a human experience. Not breaking the brand, adding texture to it.

**What sounds like Staci:**
> I used to plate food for tables of eight and watch how people reacted before they had touched anything. The first impression was set before a fork was lifted. Good design works the same way.

**What does not:**
> Design is an important factor in creating positive user experiences that drive measurable conversion outcomes.

---

## Channel Guidance

### Website Copy (Marketing Mode)

See Marketing Mode above for principles. Channel-specific notes:

- H1 and H2 headings: descriptive, keyword-informed, human. A single **bold word** for emphasis in major headings creates visual hierarchy and draws the eye.
- Hero section: problem first, solution second. Headline and subheadline under 30 words combined where possible.
- CTA text: 2-4 words, action-oriented and specific. "See our work" not "Learn more."
- Body copy: 2-3 sentences per paragraph. If a paragraph is longer, split it.
- Every section earns its place. If removing it would not hurt the page, remove it.

---

### Blog Posts (Content Mode)

- **Length:** Written to cover the topic properly. 800-1,500 words for most posts. Long enough to be useful, short enough to respect the reader's time.
- **Structure:** Hook, brief context, the useful part, low-friction CTA at the close.
- **Author:** Written in first person from Liam or Staci's perspective depending on the topic. Technical and business topics: Liam. Design, brand, UX, and human-side topics: Staci.
- **Hook:** An empathetic observation or a direct statement of the problem the post solves. Not "In this article, we will be discussing..."
- **CTA at close:** Low friction. Continue the conversation, not a hard sell. "If you are thinking through this for your own site, we are happy to take a look" not "Book a project today."
- **E-E-A-T:** First-person experience, specific examples, real observations. Write as someone who has done the thing, not someone who has read about it.

**Failure modes:**
- Throat-clearing intros: "The digital landscape is evolving rapidly...", start with the actual thing
- Over-hedging: "This might work for some businesses, but of course every situation is different...", say the thing and let the reader evaluate it
- Ending without a point, summarise the insight, then give them something to do with it

---

### LinkedIn, Manual Posts (Founder Voice)

Written by Liam or Staci from their personal accounts, in their individual voices within the Tahi brand framework.

**Liam's posts:** Direct, short, conviction-led. A blunt observation, a technical insight made accessible, or a story with a business point. One clear idea. Often no emojis. If there is a joke, it is dry and brief.

**Staci's posts:** More personal, feeling-first, relatable. An observation, a moment, a question worth sitting with. Warmer in rhythm. If there is an emoji, it feels natural to her voice, not decorative.

**Both:**
- 50-150 words for most posts
- Short paragraphs, single-sentence lines for emphasis where it earns it
- One clear takeaway
- A close that invites engagement without demanding it
- No hashtags on personal posts

**Failure modes:**
- Multiple emojis used as visual padding, if it is not adding something, cut it
- "Excited to share...", just share it
- Try-hard hooks: "Unpopular opinion:" when the opinion is not that unpopular
- Posts that end without a point or a clear takeaway
- Language that sounds like a brand post, not a person talking

**Manual post example (Liam):**

> Most Webflow performance problems are not Webflow's fault.
>
> They are third-party scripts, unoptimised images, and font stacks loading in the wrong order.
>
> The fix is usually less about adding something and more about removing things that should not have been there.
>
> Worth auditing before assuming you need a full rebuild.

**Manual post example (Staci):**

> I spent ten years cooking for other people before I designed anything.
>
> What I carried over: the best dishes are simple. Every element has a reason to be there. And if you have to explain why something is on the plate, it probably should not be.
>
> Design works the same way.

---

### LinkedIn, Auto-Generated Posts via Zapier (Tahi Brand Voice)

When a blog post publishes, Zapier generates a LinkedIn post in Tahi's brand voice. Not attributed to a specific founder. Uses the core Tahi voice: direct, clear, helpful.

**Format:**
1. **Hook**, one line that earns attention. A problem, an observation, something worth pausing for.
2. **Insight**, 2-3 lines of real value pulled from the blog. Something the reader can take away without clicking anything.
3. **Tease**, one line that makes the full post feel like the obvious next step.
4. **Link**, the blog URL.
5. **Hashtags**, 4-5, always #TahiStudio.

The goal: someone reads the post and gets something from it. Then thinks "if that was the free version, what is in the full article?" and clicks.

**Auto-post example:**

> Most Webflow sites slow down after launch, not before.
>
> It is usually not the build. It is what gets added afterwards. Marketing scripts, chat widgets, tag managers, tracking pixels. Each one costs milliseconds. Together they cost seconds.
>
> We broke down exactly what to check and what to cut.
>
> [Blog URL]
>
> #TahiStudio #Webflow #WebsitePerformance #SEO #WebDesign

**Failure modes:**
- Summarising the whole article instead of teasing it
- Vague hook: "We wrote something interesting today...", say the interesting thing
- Over 150 words total
- Emojis added because it feels like LinkedIn expects them

---

### Sales Emails (Sales Mode)

See Sales Mode above for the full framework. Channel-specific rules:

- **Greeting:** "Hey [Name]," always.
- **Sign-off:** "Cheers," always. Then the name.
- **Length:** Under 150 words. If longer, keep editing.
- **No bullet lists** in conversational emails. Bullets in formal proposals only.
- **No dashes of any kind.** No em dashes, en dashes, or double dashes ( -- ). Restructure the sentence.
- **One ask per email.** Not "let me know if you have questions, or would you like to jump on a call, or I can send more info..."
- **Read it out loud before sending.** If you would not say it, cut it.

---

### Cold Outreach (Sales Mode)

- Specific over generic. Reference something real about them.
- Lead with an observation or value, not an introduction.
- The ask is small: a reply, a yes or no, a 15-minute call. Not a project commitment.
- Do not automate without genuine personalisation. A name swap is not personalisation.

See Sales Mode examples above.

---

### Partnership Outreach (Partnership Mode)

See Partnership Mode above. Channel-specific notes:

- Shorter than sales emails. These are between peers.
- Establish mutual context in one line, not three paragraphs.
- The ask can be loose: "worth a call?" is enough.
- One follow-up is fine. Two is the limit before leaving it.

---

### Client Comms (Partnership Mode, Warmer)

These are people we are already working with. Same principles as Partnership Mode, but the relationship is established.

- Banter is fine with clients you know well. Read the relationship.
- Project updates should be proactive, not reactive. Do not wait to be asked.
- If something has gone wrong, say it immediately. Explain what happened and what is being done about it. Direct, not diplomatic.
- "Hope you are having a good week" is fine occasionally. Not as the opener of every email.

**Client comms example, project update:**

> Hey [Name],
>
> Quick update. The first pass on the homepage is done and live in your dashboard for review.
>
> Really happy with how the hero section came together. Looking forward to your thoughts.
>
> Cheers,
> [Name]

**Client comms example, raising an issue:**

> Hey [Name],
>
> Need to flag something. The third-party integration is not behaving the way we expected in production. Not a blocker, but it is going to add 2-3 days to get it right.
>
> Better to tell you now than deliver something that is not ready. Happy to talk through it if useful.
>
> Cheers,
> [Name]

---

## Email Scenarios

These are the six situations that come up most often in sales and client comms. Each one has a worked example. Follow the same principles: no dashes of any kind, under 150 words, one ask, close with Cheers.

The signature handles your name, the booking link handles scheduling. Do not offer availability by listing days. Let the link do the work.

---

### Inbound Inquiry Response

Someone has made first contact. Lead with relevance to what they described, not a generic introduction. If their inquiry gives you something specific to respond to, respond to it immediately.

> Subject: Re: [their subject line]
>
> Hey [Name],
>
> Thanks for reaching out. [Specific observation or acknowledgement of what they described, e.g. "The rebuild you're describing for your membership site is exactly the kind of project we do well."]
>
> [One sentence with a relevant credential or recent example, only if it genuinely fits.]
>
> Worth a quick call to figure out if we're the right fit? [booking link]
>
> Cheers,

**What to avoid:** "We are a small studio..." unless lean costs are genuinely relevant to what they said. Generic opening lines. Describing yourself before you have responded to them.

---

### Proposal Follow-up (Quiet After Sending)

A proposal has gone out and you have not heard back in a few days. The goal is a reply, not pressure.

> Hey [Name],
>
> Not sure if the timing has shifted on your end, or if something in the proposal needs clarifying. Either is completely fine.
>
> Happy to walk through anything. Grab a time here if a call is easier: [booking link]
>
> Cheers,

**What to avoid:** "Just circling back" or "Just following up." Say what you actually mean. One follow-up like this is appropriate. A second is the limit.

---

### Project-to-Retainer Conversion

A project has wrapped and the client is happy. The goal is to introduce ongoing support without it feeling like a pitch.

> Hey [Name],
>
> Now that [project name] is live, wanted to raise something worth thinking about.
>
> A lot of the value from a new site comes from what happens after launch: updates, optimisations, keeping things sharp. We offer ongoing support to clients we've already worked with, and you'd get the 10% loyalty discount that would normally go to a referrer.
>
> Worth a conversation? [booking link]
>
> Cheers,

**What to avoid:** Launching into pricing in the first email. Let the conversation happen on the call.

---

### Referral Programme (Telling an Existing Client It Exists)

Letting a happy client know about the referral arrangement. Keep it short, no pressure, no obligation.

> Hey [Name],
>
> Quick one. If you ever come across someone who could use what we do, there is a referral arrangement worth knowing about: 10% of any project fee goes back to you automatically via Stripe. No paperwork, no chasing.
>
> Thought it was worth mentioning.
>
> Cheers,

**What to avoid:** Making it feel transactional or like you are asking them to do your marketing. Frame it as something they should know exists, not a request.

---

### Testimonial or Case Study Ask

Two to four weeks after a project wraps and the client is visibly happy. Keep the ask easy and give them two options so they do not have to decide how to approach it.

> Hey [Name],
>
> [Project name] has been live for a few weeks now. Hope it's doing what it should.
>
> If you've been happy with how it all went, we'd genuinely appreciate a testimonial. Two easy options: reply to this with answers to two or three short questions, or I draft something based on the project and you approve it.
>
> Either works. No pressure either way.
>
> Cheers,

**Timing note:** Do not ask too early. Wait until the client has had time to see the site working. See also: **10-client-experience-and-delivery.md** for the full testimonial process.

---

### Lead Gone Quiet (Final Touch)

A lead has gone cold after a good conversation or a proposal. One final email, no pressure, leaves the door open.

> Hey [Name],
>
> Not sure if the timing has shifted or if you went a different direction. Either is completely fine.
>
> If it's still on your radar and you'd like to revisit, just reply here. If not, no follow-up from me.
>
> Cheers,

**What to avoid:** More than one email of this kind. If there is no response to this, leave it.

---

## Internal Comms

How we talk to each other reflects the same values, transparent, direct, no politics.

- Primary platform: Slack. Open channels preferred over private where possible.
- Direct feedback is encouraged. It targets the work, not the person.
- Questions are always welcome. No question is too basic.
- If something is going wrong, say it early. We are not managing each other's feelings at the expense of the work.
- No gossip in private channels. If it would not survive being said openly, it should not be said.

---

## Notes

<!-- SEO and AEO keyword guidance lives in: 15-seo-and-aeo-strategy.md, do not duplicate here. -->

<!-- Edge cases, evolving guidance, and anything that does not fit the sections above goes here. -->

<!-- POSITIONAL CHANGE LOG -->
<!-- v3.0, March 2026: Full rewrite. Four modes, founder voices, channel guidance, grammar and style rules established. -->
<!-- v3.1, March 2026: Double dash rule added explicitly to Grammar and Style, Sales Mode failure modes, and Sales Emails channel section. Proposal follow-up example updated to use calendar booking link. Email Scenarios section added with six worked examples: inbound inquiry, proposal follow-up, project-to-retainer, referral programme, testimonial ask, lead gone quiet. -->
<!-- v3.2, March 2026: Hashtag policy updated. Liam and Staci personal posts carry no hashtags. Hashtags remain on Tahi company LinkedIn page auto-generated posts only. Examples updated to reflect this. -->

# 02b: Liam's Two Writing Modes
*Version 1.1, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: 02-tone-of-voice.md, 13-marketing-strategy.md, 14-content-guidelines.md*

---

## What This Document Is

Liam writes in two distinct modes depending on where and why he is writing. This document defines both clearly so there is no ambiguity.

The Tahi brand voice (written by anyone, not just Liam) lives in 02-tone-of-voice.md. This document is specifically about Liam as an author.

---

## The Two Modes

**Mode 1: Liam as Tahi Author**
Used when Liam writes on behalf of Tahi. This includes blog posts, emails sent from business@tahi.studio, content for the Tahi website, case studies written from Liam's perspective, and company LinkedIn posts authored by Liam.

**Mode 2: Liam as Individual**
Used only on Liam's personal LinkedIn. This voice belongs to Liam the person, not Liam the co-founder. It does not represent Tahi and should not appear in any Tahi-branded material.

**The simple rule:** If it is published under Tahi or sent from a Tahi channel, use Mode 1. If it is posted from Liam's personal LinkedIn account, use Mode 2. There is no third option.

---

## Mode 1: Liam as Tahi Author

### When It Is Used

- Blog posts on the Tahi website (authored by Liam)
- Emails from business@tahi.studio, signed by Liam
- The welcome email sequence
- Case studies written from Liam's point of view
- Company LinkedIn posts where Liam is the named author
- Any Tahi content that uses "I" but means Liam as co-founder

### What It Sounds Like

Liam as Tahi author uses the Tahi brand voice as the foundation but delivers it through Liam's specific background and perspective. The reader should feel like they are hearing from a real person, not a brand, but a real person who is also representing something larger than himself.

Liam's background shapes this voice. He is an ex-Webflow full-stack developer who now works across strategy, marketing, and client relationships. He has opinions formed from real experience, not theory. He writes from that position.

**Honest and direct.** Makes points plainly without softening them into mush. Does not hedge everything. Does not pretend things are simpler than they are.

**First person but inclusive.** Uses "I" for personal experience and observations. Uses "we" when talking about what Tahi does or has built. The reader should always know whether Liam is speaking personally or for the company.

**Considered structure.** Paragraphs flow. Ideas connect. Unlike the personal LinkedIn mode, there is no stacking of single-sentence paragraphs as a stylistic device. The writing is allowed to breathe and develop an argument.

**Warm without being gushing.** Liam as Tahi author is approachable and human. Not formal. Not stiff. But also not aggressively casual. There is warmth in the honesty, not in performative friendliness.

**Occasionally blunt.** One or two sharper observations per piece are fine and are part of what makes Liam's authored content recognisably his. But bluntness serves the reader, not Liam's image.

**Soft CTAs when appropriate.** This mode has a purpose beyond documentation. It is building trust and moving readers toward Tahi over time. A natural link to case studies or a mention of the referral programme at the end of an email is appropriate. Hard sells are not.

**Tahi grammar and style rules apply.** NZ English. Title case for subject lines and headlines. No em dashes or en dashes. No exclamation marks except in genuine contexts.

### What It Does Not Sound Like

No marketing language. "Excited to share", "thrilled to announce", "passionate about" are banned in both modes.

Not ultra-short and punchy throughout. That is Mode 2. Mode 1 has rhythm but not the staccato pace of a LinkedIn post.

Not a brand document. Even though this is Tahi's channel, it sounds like a person. It does not sound like copy written by a committee.

### Example: Same Topic, Two Modes

Topic: why consistency beats first impressions in client work.

**Mode 1 (email from Liam, Tahi channel):**

> Here is something I have noticed across years of client work.
>
> A new engagement always gets the best of everyone. Over-delivered, hyper-responsive, going above and beyond. Then two months in, an existing client starts getting slower replies because a new lead came in.
>
> The clients who have stayed with Tahi for years, who refer us to others, who upgraded rather than churned, they did not stay because we impressed them at the start. They stayed because we showed up the same way in month twelve as we did in month one.
>
> Consistency is the actual differentiator. Worth keeping in mind if your business runs on referrals.

**Mode 2 (Liam's personal LinkedIn):**

> freelancers constantly over-deliver in week one.
>
> hyper-responsive. going above and beyond. making a great first impression.
>
> then month two arrives and an existing client gets slower replies because a new lead came in.
>
> the clients who stayed with us for years didn't stay because we were impressive at the start.
>
> they stayed because we showed up the same way in month twelve as we did in month one.
>
> consistency is the differentiator. not the pitch.

Same observation. Different register. Mode 1 is considered and warm. Mode 2 is compressed and raw.

---

## Mode 2: Liam as Individual (LinkedIn Playbook)

### When It Is Used

Only on Liam's personal LinkedIn account. Nowhere else.

---

### Voice

**Edgy and confident.** Liam is not performing humility. He has made real money, built real things, and has opinions that come from experience. That confidence shows. "I've made more money in my undies than I did in a uniform" is the right energy. Not arrogant, but not apologetic either.

**Honest about the shit parts.** The unglamorous reality of running a business is fair game — no desk for five weeks, working from a waiting room, NZ delivery times keeping you humble. But these stories exist to make a point, not to project misery. Liam enjoys his work. He chose the hard parts. The framing is "chosen suffering" (the person who loves the game, not the martyr who hates their life).

**Occasionally foul-mouthed.** Swearing is fine when it lands the point. "The 'free' option is a lie" works. "The 'free' option is bullshit" works harder. Use when it adds emphasis, not decoration.

**Short sentences. Punchy rhythm.** One idea per sentence, sometimes per line. Justin Welsh structure: each line earns the next. The weight comes from compression.

**Analogy-first.** The Corolla vs Ferrari line is the reason the WordPress post travelled. "Popularity doesn't equal performance" is the idea. The analogy is what people share. Every post benefits from one good analogy.

**NZ-authentic.** "Flick me a DM." "Yeah, not bad." The Tall Poppy post worked because it named a cultural truth. Liam is a New Zealander working globally and that specificity is interesting, not limiting.

**Observation, not accusation.** The hook is a statement about how things are, not an attack on the reader. "Here is the difference between a freelancer and an agency" lands. "Your in-house dev is the bottleneck" puts the reader on the back foot before they even start reading. One is curious; one is defensive.

**Stories can be lightly fictionalised.** If the scenario is representative of something real, it can be told slightly dramatised. It needs to be believable, not literally true. The dentist laptop scenario works because everyone recognises that version of work pressure, whether or not it happened exactly that way.

**First person throughout.** "I" always. "We" only when referring to Tahi specifically.

---

### The 5 Formats

Rotate across these. Defaulting to one is how posts go stale. Assign a format to every post before writing it.

---

**Format 1: Educational / Value-Driven**

Give away something genuinely useful. The more specific and practical, the better. Best when it is technical, counterintuitive, or saves the reader time. The attribution code post (60 comments, "want the code? drop a comment") is the gold standard.

Structure:
- Open with a bold specific claim or observation
- Deliver the value in the body (tips, code, framework, breakdown)
- End with a low-friction CTA: "drop a comment", "link below", "flick me a DM"

Examples of what works here: Webflow code snippets, CRO test ideas, frameworks for pricing conversations, productivity observations backed by experience.

Examples that do not work here: generic lists of things everyone already knows, "5 tips for X" without a sharp angle.

CTA: "want it? comment below" or "drop a comment and I'll send it over" or "link in the comments"

---

**Format 2: Personal Story / Unglamorous Reality**

Share the part of the work nobody photographs. Works because it is relatable and most people are too polished to post it. The couch/no desk post got 7,826 impressions. The dentist laptop scenario is the template.

Structure:
- Open mid-scene (not "I want to tell you about the time...")
- The unglamorous detail is the hook
- The lesson is buried in the middle or at the end, not announced
- End with a question that invites people to share their version

The rule: the story exists to make a point, not to document suffering. "My back hurts. But the work got shipped." is the whole philosophy in two sentences.

Can be partly fictional but must be entirely believable.

CTA: a question at the end. "What's your setup — end game or work in progress?" "What's the weirdest place you've ever worked from?"

---

**Format 3: Contrarian / Hot Take**

Challenge the conventional wisdom in the industry. Works best when Liam has data, an analogy, or a specific argument. Not just "here is my opinion" but "here is why you are wrong and here is my evidence."

The WordPress posts are the model: highest performing content in the account. The key ingredients:
- Acknowledge what everyone thinks ("WordPress is free. Technically true.")
- Signal you are about to disagree ("But let's be honest about where it actually fits.")
- Back it with specifics (total cost of ownership breakdown, the plugin tax, time to market)
- Land it with an analogy (Corolla vs Ferrari)
- End with a clear conclusion

Doubling down on previous controversy works. "I kicked the hornets' nest a couple of weeks ago" is a perfect opener because it rewards the people who commented on the first post and invites new readers in.

Never back down in the post itself. The confidence is the point.

CTA: invite the debate. "Agree?" "Come at me in the comments." Or let the controversy generate its own conversation.

---

**Format 4: Comparison / Framework**

Pick two or three things. Compare them in the sharpest possible terms. End on the most interesting one. The in-house / freelancer / agency post got 12,887 impressions from roughly 50 words.

Structure:
- "Here is the difference between X, Y, and Z."
- One line per item. No padding.
- The last line lands the hardest.

This format benefits from having a slightly unexpected third option or a twist ending. It also works well when readers immediately categorise themselves ("which one am I?").

No CTA needed. The comparison is the whole thing.

---

**Format 5: Milestone / Community / Social Proof**

Wins, announcements, and community moments. Works when framed as a cultural observation or community invitation rather than self-promotion. The Tall Poppy post works because it names a cultural truth (NZ conditioning) and weaponises the discomfort. The Webflow Leader post works because it immediately pivots to community (meetups, who is in NZ?).

A list of wins alone lands flat. A list of wins framed around "why I almost did not post this" lands differently.

When posting milestones: give the discomfort or the context that makes it interesting. Then give the win. Then open it up to the community.

CTA: community invitation, event sign-up, follow, or just an open question.

---

### Hook Principles

The first line is the only line most people read before deciding whether to click. Everything else is irrelevant if the hook fails.

**What works:**
- Challenge a common assumption: "People think you need a $2,000 chair to build a business."
- Promise a clear comparison: "Here is the difference between an in-house team, a freelancer, and an agency."
- Immediate contradiction: "WordPress is free. Technically true."
- Specific outrageous claim: "I have a bit of code that increases attribution by 60,000%."
- Emotional signal: "This post makes my skin crawl."
- Self-referential callback: "I kicked the hornets' nest a couple of weeks ago."

**What does not work:**
- Accusatory second-person hooks: "Are you paying for your agency's overhead?" puts the reader on defence before they've read anything.
- Vague wisdom: "Your best work happens because of what you do when you aren't working." True, forgettable.
- Announcement energy: "I'm excited to share..." Skip entirely.

The pattern: statement and observation hooks outperform question and accusation hooks, even when the target is the ICP. Make them curious, not defensive.

---

### Post Anatomy

Every post has three parts, even if each is only one line.

**Hook.** The first line. The whole job is to earn the second line. Not to set up the pitch, not to introduce yourself. Earn the scroll.

**Body.** The actual value, story, argument, or framework. One idea per line. No filler sentences. If a line does not add to the post, cut it. The Justin Welsh test: read each line and ask "does this earn the next?"

**Ending.** Either a landing statement (the point, compressed) or a question that invites a response. Not both. Not a paragraph of conclusion. The ending should feel like the post clicking shut.

---

### CTA Guide

Not every post needs a CTA. The format determines it.

| Format | CTA type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Educational / Value | Low-friction action | "Want the code? Drop a comment." / "Link in comments." |
| Personal Story | Conversational question | "What's the weirdest place you've worked from?" |
| Contrarian / Hot Take | Debate invite or none | "Agree? Come at me." / let it run |
| Comparison / Framework | Usually none | The post is the CTA |
| Milestone / Community | Community action | "Who's in NZ and would come along?" / "Follow for more." |

For ICP-targeted posts: the CTA can be a soft DM invite, but only after the post has delivered genuine value first. "If you're ready to stop managing the people you pay, DM me" only lands if the post before it made the reader feel understood, not sold to.

---

### ICP vs Peers

Currently 80/20 toward peers. Target is closer to 50/50 or blurred lines.

The mistake in recent ICP posts is writing from the sales perspective rather than the reader's perspective. "Your in-house dev is the bottleneck" is Liam describing a sales scenario. "Your in-house team clocks out at 5:01. A freelancer grinds the weekend. Here's where an agency actually earns its cost" is Liam describing something the ICP recognises.

Same audience. Completely different engagement.

**When targeting peers (freelancers, agency owners, developers):**
Format 1, 2, 3, and 4 all work well. The reader is a peer so they respond to things that make them feel seen, smarter, or slightly called out in a way they enjoy.

**When targeting ICP (business owners, marketing teams, founders):**
Format 3 and 4 work best. The ICP is not there to learn about freelancing. They respond to posts that describe their own problems in sharper terms than they could, or that challenge the assumptions they are making about their website, their platform, or their agency. "We make ourselves redundant" is the best ICP CTA Liam has written — it demonstrates confidence in the work and removes the fear of dependency. More like that.

ICP content that does not feel like a pitch can convert quietly. Someone who read the WordPress cost breakdown, then the agency comparison post, then a results post is much warmer by the time they reach out than someone who got hit with three DM-me posts in a row.

---

### Format Rules

**Line breaks.** Single-sentence paragraphs are standard. Use them as a reader would pause. Never bury two ideas in one paragraph.

**Length.** No fixed rule. The default is short: 80 to 200 words. But break the mold when the content earns it. A contrarian argument with data and analogy can run 400+ words and outperform a 60-word post. A single sharp comparison can be 30 words and get 12,000 impressions. Let the content determine the length, not the calendar.

**Lowercase openers.** Used selectively. Signals a thought in progress. Do not let it become a default style — it loses its effect.

**Emojis.** Sparing and purposeful. 📌 for pinned comments. 👇 before a question when it adds rhythm. Maximum two in a post body. Never mid-sentence. Never as decoration.

**Hashtags.** Three maximum. Only if genuinely relevant. #Webflow and #WebDesign are worth including on technical posts for discovery. Skip on opinion and story posts where they break the tone.

**Images.** Use when they add context, not when they fill a box. A photo of the couch office works. A stock image of a laptop does not. Results screenshots, before/after, and real photos all outperform brand imagery.

**Pinned comment.** Use it. Post a pinned comment with 📌 and two to three sentences: either the distilled insight from the post, a relevant link, or a follow-up CTA. Sebastian Bimbi's approach: "📌 The hardest part of freelancing isn't the work. It's that the work never stops." The pinned comment is the thing someone shares or screenshots.

---

### What Never to Do

Do not write a post that is primarily a pitch. The post earns the CTA. If the body is just setup for "DM me", the body is not doing its job.

Do not write accusatory hooks targeting the ICP. "You're doing X wrong" is less effective than "Here is why X costs more than you think."

Do not use this channel to push Tahi marketing. Liam's personal profile can mention Tahi, link to things, and talk about what they are building. It cannot become a feed of Tahi announcements. Audience trust erodes when a personal account becomes a brand account.

Do not project negativity. The shit parts are the parts Liam chose. The framing is always: I know what this costs and I am still in. Not: everything is hard and thankless.

Do not use marketing language. "Excited to share", "thrilled to announce", "passionate about" are banned. They signal that a brand wrote this, not a person.

Do not post the selfie with the cactus to boost reach on a technical post. The value should be in the advice.

---

### Topics

Post about whatever is relevant, interesting, or timely. The mood determines the post. Some examples of territory that works:

Webflow, Framer, platform comparisons, and the stack decisions behind them. Agency and freelancer dynamics, pricing, retainers, and what makes client work sustainable. CRO, web performance, and things that actually move conversion numbers. Business building, going full-time, hitting financial milestones, and the NZ-specific cultural layer around all of it. The unglamorous side: no desk, working from airports, the gap between the dream and the Tuesday afternoon. AI tools and what they actually change (not hype, real use). Tahi milestones and what they mean in practice. Observations that come from a post going viral or a comment section going sideways.

When using AI to generate ideas: give it the format, the target audience (peers or ICP), and one seed topic or observation. It should return the hook, the structure, and the body. The voice check: would Liam say this, or does it sound like a LinkedIn content ghost? Cut anything that sounds like a motivational poster.

---

## Quick Reference

| | Mode 1: Liam as Tahi Author | Mode 2: Liam as Individual |
|---|---|---|
| **Where** | Blog, Tahi emails, company LinkedIn | Personal LinkedIn only |
| **Voice base** | Tahi brand voice, delivered personally | Liam's own voice — edgy, confident, honest |
| **Sentence style** | Varied, considered, flows | Short, punchy, one idea per line |
| **Pronouns** | I (personal) and we (Tahi) | I always, we only when referring to Tahi |
| **Structure** | Paragraphs develop ideas | Hook → body → landing/question |
| **CTAs** | Soft CTAs appropriate | Format-dependent — story posts end with questions, value posts end with actions |
| **Tone** | Warm, direct, honest | Confident, occasionally edgy, unglamorous when earned |
| **Capitalisation** | Title case for headlines, NZ English | Sentence case, lowercase openers used selectively |
| **Emojis** | None | Sparing: 📌 pinned comments, 👇 before questions |
| **Length** | Standard long-form | Default short (80–200 words), break the mold when earned |
| **Swearing** | Never | Okay when it adds emphasis, not decoration |
| **Planning** | Can be planned ahead | Format and topic can be planned, execution stays fresh |
| **Purpose** | Build trust toward Tahi | Build Liam's authority, attract peers and ICP |

---

## Notes

<!-- POSITIONAL CHANGE LOG -->
<!-- v1.0, March 2026: First version. Covered Liam's personal LinkedIn voice only. -->
<!-- v1.1, March 2026: Restructured to cover both writing modes. Added Mode 1 (Liam as Tahi Author) to address gap identified during welcome email sequence drafting. Side-by-side examples added. Quick reference table added. -->
<!-- v1.2, March 2026: Mode 2 completely rewritten as a full LinkedIn playbook. Added: the 5 post formats with examples from real posts, hook principles with what works/doesn't (backed by post performance data), post anatomy, CTA guide by format, ICP vs peers guidance, pinned comment strategy, emoji guidance, and topics list. Updated quick reference table to match. -->
