pages.content:
# 06: Brand Guidelines
*Version 3.0, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: 01-brand-dna.md, 02-tone-of-voice.md*

---

## What This Document Is

The visual half of the brand. Colours, type, shape language, UI patterns, and the assets that show up across every surface. For the brand values and positioning that sit behind these decisions, see 01-brand-dna.md.

The visual identity is built around one idea: nature expressed through precision. Organic shapes, green tones, growth motifs, delivered with clean geometry and tight rules. It should feel calm, considered, and alive at the same time.

---

## Colour Palette

### Brand Green

The primary colour. Used for the logo, the leaf icon, key CTAs, and standout words in headings.

| Token | HEX | Usage |
|---|---|---|
| Brand Green | `#5A824E` | Logo, leaf icon, heading accents, dark mode CTA |
| Brand Green Dark | `#425F39` | Gradient endpoint for the leaf icon |

---

### Light Mode

| Role | HEX | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Text | `#121A0F` | Near-black with a green undertone |
| Background | `#FFFFFF` | Pure white |
| Border | `#CDCFCC` | Neutral light grey |
| CTA | `#78C45E` | Brighter green for buttons and primary actions |

---

### Dark Mode

| Role | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Text | `#FFFFFF` | Pure white |
| Background | `#2A3626` | Deep forest green |
| Border | `#FFFFFF` at 20% opacity | Subtle, low-contrast |
| CTA | `#5A824E` | Brand green, darker read on the deep bg |

---

## Typography

**Typeface:** Manrope. Used exclusively across all UI, headings, body copy, and interface elements.

Manrope is a geometric sans-serif. It reads cleanly at small sizes and has enough character at large sizes to carry a heading without decoration. No secondary typeface. Manrope handles everything.

**Heading treatment:** In headings, one or two standout words are set in Brand Green (`#5A824E`) to create emphasis and draw the eye. The rest of the heading stays in the default text colour. This is applied selectively, not to every heading. The goal is one moment of colour per heading block, not a pattern that becomes noise.

---

## Shape Language

### The Leaf Shape

The defining geometric motif. Elements use a selective border radius that creates a subtle leaf shape:

- **Bottom-left corner:** `16px` (or `1rem`) radius
- **Top-right corner:** `16px` (or `1rem`) radius
- **Top-left corner:** Sharp (`0px`)
- **Bottom-right corner:** Sharp (`0px`)

This applies to cards, containers, input fields, image frames, and any other element where a border radius is appropriate. The effect is intentional and restrained. At 16px it reads as a design choice, not an accident.

In CSS shorthand: `border-radius: 0 16px 0 16px;`

The order is: top-left, top-right, bottom-right, bottom-left.

---

## Icons and Marks

### Leaf Icon

Used in taglines, alongside brand statements, and as an inline accent. The gradient runs from Brand Green to the darker endpoint, giving it depth.

```svg
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="12" height="15" viewBox="0 0 12 15" fill="none">
  <path d="M11.3891 4.28558C11.2851 5.96398 10.8801 7.61685 10.0865 9.10734C8.33699 12.3912 5.05681 13.9875 1.5577 14.7774C2.61765 11.448 4.62078 8.34294 7.00156 5.80162C7.05264 5.74689 7.10373 5.69033 7.15481 5.63195L7.15298 5.6356C6.94136 5.95304 6.53818 6.1227 6.24629 6.35257C5.86317 6.65541 5.51107 7.01116 5.16991 7.35779C3.69766 8.84828 2.53555 10.6106 1.53763 12.4441C1.30412 12.8746 1.0779 13.3088 0.862622 13.7485C0.765931 13.9455 0.661945 14.1426 0.579849 14.3469C0.517821 14.4983 0.368216 14.8358 0.433892 15C0.371864 14.6899 0.302548 14.3797 0.249642 14.0696C-0.157188 11.6833 -0.151724 9.12741 0.913698 6.9017C1.63614 5.39114 2.81102 4.12686 4.16287 3.13806C6.16418 1.67493 8.56868 0.706202 10.9349 0.00929929C11.0662 -0.0290121 11.0662 0.0585568 11.0808 0.13518C11.3545 1.4852 11.4731 2.89542 11.3873 4.28558H11.3891Z" fill="url(#paint0_linear_8023_6098)"/>
  <defs>
    <linearGradient id="paint0_linear_8023_6098" x1="-0.086051" y1="7.57671" x2="11.681" y2="7.57671" gradientUnits="userSpaceOnUse">
      <stop stop-color="#5A824E"/>
      <stop offset="1" stop-color="#425F39"/>
    </linearGradient>
  </defs>
</svg>
```

---

### Tick Mark

Used in feature lists, benefit callouts, and anywhere a checkmark is needed. Set in Brand Green.

```svg
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="17" viewBox="0 0 24 17" fill="none">
  <path d="M1.33398 8.87435L7.93365 15.474L22.077 1.33203" stroke="#5A824E" stroke-width="2.66667" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
</svg>
```

---

### Circle Background Element

A large decorative ring used as a subtle background element. It appears at 20 to 60 percent opacity, layered behind content to add depth without competing with it. Common on hero sections and full-bleed dark panels.

The element is a donut shape: a large filled circle with a cutout, creating a thick ring. Used in Brand Green. Opacity is set on the element or its wrapper depending on context.

```svg
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="590" height="729" viewBox="0 0 590 729" fill="none">
  <path fill-rule="evenodd" clip-rule="evenodd" d="M225.5 79.2391C67.9548 79.2391 -59.7609 206.955 -59.7609 364.5C-59.7609 522.045 67.9548 649.761 225.5 649.761C383.045 649.761 510.761 522.045 510.761 364.5C510.761 206.955 383.045 79.2391 225.5 79.2391ZM-139 364.5C-139 163.192 24.1922 0 225.5 0C426.808 0 590 163.192 590 364.5C590 565.808 426.808 729 225.5 729C24.1922 729 -139 565.808 -139 364.5Z" fill="#5A824E"/>
</svg>
```

Typical usage: `opacity: 0.2` to `opacity: 0.6`. Scale as needed. Partially cropped at the canvas edge is intentional and preferred over the full circle being visible.

---

## UI Patterns

### Glass Morphism (Dark Backgrounds)

Used on dark backgrounds for elements like buttons, step titles, and feature callouts. The effect is a translucent green layer with a pill shape, using an asymmetric radius that mirrors the leaf shape language at a rounder scale.

```css
display: flex;
padding: 24px 32px;
flex-direction: column;
justify-content: center;
align-items: flex-start;
gap: 10px;
border-radius: 99999px 99999px 99999px 99999px;
background: rgba(53, 77, 46, 0.20);
```

The background value `rgba(53, 77, 46, 0.20)` is a dark green at 20% opacity. This creates the glass effect against the dark mode background. The border radius is fully rounded (pill shape) in this pattern, which contrasts intentionally with the asymmetric leaf shape used elsewhere. Use it selectively: step indicators, subtle button variants, decorative content labels on dark sections.

---

## What Not to Do

These are the most common ways the visual identity breaks down.

The heading colour accent should be one or two words per heading, not half the sentence. When too many words are green, the effect disappears.

The leaf shape radius applies to elements where a border radius is appropriate. It does not apply to everything. Circles, avatars, and pill buttons have their own radius logic and should not be forced into the leaf shape.

The circle background element should feel atmospheric. If it is fully visible and centred, it reads as a graphic rather than a background texture. Crop it, offset it, and keep the opacity low.

The glass morphism style is for dark sections. On a white background it loses the effect entirely and looks like a faded box. Do not use it in light mode.

Manrope is the only typeface. Do not introduce system fonts or second typefaces for any reason, including email clients or documents. When Manrope is not available in a specific context (plain text email, for example), fall back to the system sans-serif and do not try to replicate the styled version.

---

## Still to Define

The following are not yet documented and will be added when decided:

- Logo variants (full lockup, icon only, reversed, minimum sizes, clear space rules)
- Illustration and photography style
- Motion and animation principles
- Presentation and document templates

---

## Notes

<!-- POSITIONAL CHANGE LOG -->
<!-- v3.0, March 2026: Full rewrite from old Brand Bible scaffold. Colour palette (light and dark modes), typography (Manrope), leaf shape language, leaf icon, tick mark, circle background element, glass morphism pattern, heading colour treatment, and What Not to Do all documented for the first time. -->

pages.title: Services and Pricing
pages.content:
# 07: Services & Pricing
*Version 3.0, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: 04-ideal-client-profile.md, 08-financial-overview.md*
*Referenced by: 09-sales-strategy.md, website copy*

---

## How We Work

Every engagement at Tahi runs on a track model. A track is an active lane of work, a task or project in progress. What differs between plans is how many tracks are running at once and how large a task each track can hold.

**Small track:** Tasks that take up to a day. Sections added, pages duplicated, copy updated, bugs fixed, small structural changes.

**Large track:** Tasks that take a week or more. Full page builds, SEO and AEO overhauls, major feature additions, CMS restructures, multi-day integrations.

This distinction is what keeps quality high. Instead of ten things moving slowly at once, the right number of things move properly.

---

## The Value Anchor

*This section informs how we talk about pricing in sales conversations. Not for publishing verbatim, for understanding the framing.*

When a client compares Tahi to hiring separately, the math is straightforward.

A Webflow developer alone runs $75-150/hour. At 15-20 hours a month, that is $1,100-$3,000, just for development. A UI/UX designer adds another $600-$1,500. Analytics setup and attribution management from a specialist: $500-$2,000/month ongoing. CRM integration as a standalone project: $2,000-$8,000. SEO/AEO implementation from a specialist agency: $1,000-$3,000/month. Add a CRO consultant when conversion is the problem: another $1,500-$4,000/month. Total from separate specialists: $5,200-$17,500+ per month, with the coordination overhead of managing four to five different relationships.

The Maintain plan is $1,500. The Scale plan is $4,000. That gap versus the alternative is the conversation.

In sales, lead with what they get. Stack it. Then name the price. The price should feel like the obvious conclusion, not the negotiation.

---

## Custom Projects

For businesses that need a defined scope, a deadline, and a clear deliverable. This covers migrations, full relaunches, redesigns, and lighter finishing work.

**When custom projects make sense:**
- A complete redesign or migration from WordPress or another platform
- A deadline-driven build that needs scoping upfront
- Finishing work, the last 10-40% of a build someone else started, template customisation, or polishing a near-complete site

**Tune**, from $750 USD
Smaller, tightly scoped work. Finishing touches, template builds, targeted fixes. Includes one week of post-delivery bug support.

**Launch**, from $2,500 USD
Migrations, full relaunches, overhauls, major builds. Scope, deliverables, and deadlines mapped in the dashboard before work begins. Includes two weeks of post-delivery support, giving the client a direct experience of what an ongoing retainer looks like before committing to one.

All custom projects are scoped individually. The "from" prices are the floor. Final pricing depends on scope, complexity, and timeline.

---

## Retainer Plans

*Note: Plan names below (Maintain and Scale) are the recommended naming pending final confirmation. Structure and pricing are locked; names to be applied across marketing once confirmed.*

Retainers are the core of how Tahi works. A project has a finish line. A retainer is how businesses keep improving after it. Both plans include design and development together from day one.

---

### Maintain Plan, $1,500 USD/month

**For:** Businesses with a live, functional Webflow site that want steady, quality improvement without the overhead of a full growth engagement.

**Tracks:** One small track at a time. Each task up to a day in scope, one completes before the next begins.

**Included, everything that directly touches Webflow:**
- Webflow UI/UX design and page builds
- Webflow development, code, and integrations
- Analytics and attribution (Webflow-connected)
- CRM setup and management (Webflow-connected)
- Graphic design serving the Webflow site (icons, images, section assets)
- SEO implementation (structural, on-page, technical)
- AEO implementation (structuring content for AI overview visibility)
- Dashboard access for task tracking and file review

**Not included:** Graphic design for non-Webflow assets (presentations, brand collateral, marketing materials), quarterly marketing strategy calls, large-track work, CRO. These are Scale plan territory.

**What this looks like in practice:** A landing page updated, a section added, a bug fixed, an SEO title structure overhauled, one thing done properly at a time. The site stays sharp and improving without a heavy monthly commitment.

**What the alternative costs:** A Webflow developer, a designer, and an analytics person hired separately, $3,700-$7,000/month, minimum, before coordination overhead. Maintain is $1,500.

**Upgrade trigger:** When the work that needs doing takes more than a day at a time, or when strategic marketing direction, non-Webflow design, or CRO alongside execution becomes the need, Scale is the move.

---

### Scale Plan, $4,000 USD/month

**For:** Businesses actively investing in growth. More output, more capability, and strategic input included alongside execution.

**Tracks:** Two tracks running simultaneously, one large track and one small track.

The large track holds multi-day or multi-week work. The small track runs alongside it for bugs, quick fixes, and smaller tasks that should not wait behind a bigger piece of work. Something significant is always moving, and the smaller work never stops.

**Included, everything in Maintain, plus:**
- Large track capacity (week-plus tasks running concurrently with small track)
- Graphic design beyond Webflow: presentations, pitch decks, brand collateral, marketing materials, app and product design
- CRO (Conversion Rate Optimisation), a specialist contractor is brought in as needed for CRO-focused work within the engagement. Scoped within the Scale plan; larger CRO projects priced separately
- Quarterly marketing strategy call with a senior marketing contact, reviewing what has been done, what is planned, and where to focus next
- A light roadmap to keep the next quarter directional, not reactive

**What this looks like in practice:** A structural AEO overhaul on the large track, bug fixes and section updates on the small track, a CRO pass on the primary conversion page, and a quarterly call that makes sure the next build is pointed at the right thing.

**What the alternative costs:** Everything in the Maintain comparison, plus a senior brand designer, a CRO consultant, and a marketing strategist. Separately: $7,000-$16,000/month. Scale is $4,000.

---

## Add-Ons

Two add-ons available across both plans.

**SEO and AEO Dashboard**, $150 USD/month
A reporting and tracking dashboard showing how the site performs in search and AI overviews. Separate from the implementation work included in the plan, this is the visibility layer on top of it. Useful for clients who want to see the data clearly, not just trust that the work is happening.

**Priority Support**
- Maintain plan: $1,000 USD/month, adds a second small track and faster response times
- Scale plan: $1,500 USD/month, adds a third track (any size) and faster response times

For clients who need more throughput than their base plan provides, or who have time-sensitive work that cannot wait in the normal queue.

**The full pricing ladder:**

| | Maintain | Scale |
|---|---|---|
| Base | $1,500/month | $4,000/month |
| With Priority Support | $2,500/month | $5,500/month |
| With SEO/AEO Dashboard | $1,650/month | $4,150/month |
| With both add-ons | $2,650/month | $5,650/month |

---

## Pricing Sustainability

*Internal reference. Not for client-facing use.*

Pricing is built to sustain the business at every stage, from two founders with three clients to a team of ten with fifty. The numbers need to hold at both ends.

**Tool costs (approximate monthly, shared across all clients):**
- ManyRequests, Figma, Webflow, analytics tools, SEO tools, AI tools, project management: $800-$1,500/month total. Per-client cost drops sharply as the client base grows.

**Staffing:**

Hiring is incremental and triggered by actual delivery load, not a fixed client count. The model is part-time first, full-time when sustained load justifies it, covering the role that is bottlenecking (dev, design, or PM depending on the client mix at the time). Cash buffer always before headcount.

**Effective hourly rate by plan (internal reference):**

These estimates are based on realistic delivery hours per plan including PM, communications, and QA.

| Plan | Est. hours/month | Effective rate |
|---|---|---|
| Maintain ($1,500) | ~25 hours | ~$60/hour |
| Maintain + Priority ($2,500) | ~35 hours | ~$71/hour |
| Scale ($4,000) | ~60 hours | ~$67/hour |
| Scale + Priority ($5,500) | ~75-80 hours | ~$69-73/hour |

Rates are consistent across the ladder, which confirms pricing is internally coherent. Priority Support is the highest-margin add-on relative to the delivery hours it adds, it primarily provides urgency insurance and an extra track slot, not proportional volume.

$4,000 Scale plan pricing is load-bearing for all growth math. Do not discount it.

**Custom pricing:**
Custom plans and pricing exist and will always exist for the right client. The advertised plans are the default path. Custom is for enterprise engagements that do not fit the standard scope. Custom pricing must still reflect the true cost of delivery, tools, time, and opportunity cost.

**Discount policy:**
No discounts on retainer plans are advertised or offered to new clients. If a client cannot afford the plan, the Maintain tier exists for that reason. Discounting the Scale plan devalues it and erodes the margin that makes the business sustainable. Project pricing has more flexibility, especially when the project is a door-opener to a retainer.

Quarterly and annual payment discounts (previously 10% and 20% respectively) are no longer advertised. Hourly packages and previous add-ons are also retired from the offer. These options, including pre-payment discounts, may still be extended to existing long-term clients at discretion, but are not promoted and are not the default.

---

## Ecosystem and Community Products

Beyond direct client work, Tahi is building resources for anyone who works with Webflow.

**Nodeo**, SaaS integrating with Webflow. Close to launch. Paid.

**Template library**, In progress. Free and paid Webflow templates across use cases and budgets.

**Pricing calculator**, Live. Free resource for Webflow freelancers scoping their work.

**Mentoring and education**, Ongoing. Content, direct community engagement, and paid mentoring.

**Free code snippets and clonables**, Ongoing. Free resources for anyone building in Webflow.

---

## Billing

**New and lighter clients:** ManyRequests dashboard via Stripe. Automated and low-friction.

**Established long-term clients:** Invoiced through Xero, paid directly. Avoids Stripe fees on both sides. Standard for ongoing retainer relationships.

All work, task tracking, and file delivery runs through ManyRequests regardless of billing method. Primary communication via Slack and email. ManyRequests handles task status and file review.

---

## Pricing Notes

Retainer and project prices are published in USD, reflecting the primary US and UK market. NZD equivalents apply for New Zealand and Australian clients.

All published prices are floors. Final pricing is scoped based on complexity, timeline, and what the work actually requires. We scope honestly. See: 01-brand-dna.md (We Push Back).

Detailed financial targets and growth metrics: 08-financial-overview.md.

---

## Positional Change Log

*For internal reference. Tracks where we used to stand and where we stand now.*

| Topic | Previous position | Current position | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plan names | Design Pro, Webflow Dev Focus, Total Webflow | Tune (project light), Launch (project full), Maintain ($1,500/month), Scale ($4,000/month), names pending final confirmation | Old names implied design and dev as separate options; new names describe function and intent clearly |
| Scope split | Not differentiated | Light = Webflow-only scope; Full = Webflow plus marketing, graphic design beyond Webflow, strategy calls | Creates a clear, logical upgrade path rather than just "more of the same" |
| Included services | Core subscription with most things as add-ons | Analytics, attribution, CRM, SEO/AEO all included in base plans | Positions Tahi as the full-picture partner; add-ons simplified to two |
| Value anchoring | USP statement ("refreshingly simple subscription") | Explicit comparison to hiring separately; price revealed as the obvious conclusion | Aligns with Conviction Rule and sales mode framing in TOV |
| Discount policy | Not documented | No discounts on retainer plans; project pricing has flexibility | Margin protection at every stage of growth |
| Pricing published | USD for projects only | USD for both projects and retainers; NZD for NZ/AU market | Consistency across the pricing tier |
| CRO | Not offered | Available in Scale plan via specialist contractor as needed; scoped within the plan or separately for larger CRO engagements | Extends Tahi's capability without requiring a full-time CRO hire |
| Staffing model | Not documented | Incremental hiring triggered by actual load, not fixed thresholds; first hire around 8-12 clients depending on Scale mix | Avoids premature overhead; cash buffer before headcount |
| Pre-payment discounts | 10% quarterly, 20% annual discount advertised | Discontinued as advertised offers; may be extended to existing long-term clients at discretion only | Not being used; simplifies pricing and protects margin |
| Hourly packages | Offered | Retired; no longer advertised | Not aligned with the track model or retainer-first positioning |
| Add-ons | Multiple additional add-ons offered | Simplified to two: SEO/AEO Dashboard ($150) and Priority Support ($1,000/$1,500) | Reduces decision fatigue; previous add-ons absorbed into plan scope or removed |

pages.title: Financial Overview
pages.content:
# 08: Financial Overview
*Version 3.0, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: 07-services-and-pricing.md*
*Live data lives in: finances.xlsx, do not duplicate figures here.*

---

## Purpose of This Document

This document covers the financial philosophy, targets, cost structure, and billing principles of Tahi Studio. It is not a live financial record, actual income, expenses, and client data are maintained in finances.xlsx. This document provides the context and logic behind the numbers so that financial decisions can be made consistently.

---

## How We Think About Money

Money is not the goal, it is the resource that makes the goal possible. The goal is a business that delivers excellent work, supports the people doing that work, and can run without being dependent on any one client or person.

That means we think about revenue in terms of what it enables: sustainable founder salaries, a first hire, a cash buffer that removes the anxiety from slow months, and the capacity to invest in tools and growth. We do not optimise for the highest possible revenue at the expense of the client relationships or delivery quality that make the revenue sustainable.

We track finances actively, review them regularly, and make decisions based on what the numbers actually say, not on estimates or gut feel.

---

## Revenue Targets and Milestones

All figures in NZD unless otherwise noted.

**Current stage:** $10,000-$15,000 per month recurring. The business is currently in a transition period following the churn of a significant client. Revenue is being actively rebuilt.

**First milestone, $30,000 per month:** This is the minimum viable target for the current cost structure at full capacity. At this level, the business can support both founder salaries at the target rate, a first full-time hire, tools, and meaningful buffer contribution. It is not a comfortable plateau, it is the floor for the structure we are building toward.

**Second milestone, $50,000 per month:** Comfortable operations with the full team, meaningful profit, and capacity to invest further in growth.

**Long-term target, $100,000 per month:** Requires a larger team, systems that run without constant founder input, and a client base that is diversified across regions and industries.

Progress toward the $30,000 target is tracked monthly in finances.xlsx.

---

## Salary Policy

Both co-founders currently draw $52,000 NZD per year. The target salary is $74,000 NZD per year each. Salary increases are tied to revenue milestones, not to a calendar date.

The first full-time hire is budgeted at $100,000 NZD per year. This is the primary reason the $30,000 monthly revenue milestone is meaningful, it is the number at which that hire becomes financially viable alongside the salary increase. See: 03-team-and-structure.md for the hiring rationale.

---

## Cash Buffer Policy

We target six months of operating costs held as a cash buffer. At current cost levels, six months of costs is approximately $65,000-$75,000 NZD. As costs increase (with the hire and salary adjustment), the buffer target increases proportionally.

The buffer is not an emergency fund for bad months, it is a structural commitment to not making panicked decisions. It means we can afford to turn down a bad-fit client, wait for the right hire, or absorb a client churning without it becoming a crisis.

Current buffer: approximately $30,000 NZD. This covers roughly 2.5 months of current operating costs. Growing the buffer to the six-month target is a standing financial priority alongside revenue growth.

---

## Cost Structure

Full expense detail lives in finances.xlsx. The categories are:

**Salaries** are the largest cost. Both founder salaries together are $8,667 NZD per month at current rates. This will increase when the salary milestone is hit, and will roughly double again when the first hire is made.

**Tools and subscriptions** run at approximately $2,500 NZD per month including all software, platforms, and services. The most significant tool costs are SE Ranking (see below), the Beany accountant, Google Workspace, office expenses, and ManyRequests.

**Growth spend** includes any active investment in lead generation or marketing. The StraightIn LinkedIn outreach agency is the current example ($900 USD per month, February through June 2026). Growth spend is evaluated on a contract-by-contract basis against the leads and revenue it generates. It does not automatically renew.

**Hosted client sites** include Webflow plan costs for client sites managed under the agency workspace. One site (Play Therapy) is hosted as a personal favour at no charge to the client. All commercial client sites are either billed directly to the client or the hosting cost is factored into the engagement.

---

## SE Ranking, Cost and Revenue Model

SE Ranking costs approximately £164 per month (roughly $344 NZD). We charge clients $150 USD per month for access to the SE Ranking dashboard as part of certain retainer plans.

At one client on the dashboard: marginal loss. At two clients: roughly break-even. At three or more clients: net positive margin. The tool is currently generating margin, not just covering its cost.

Dashboard access has also driven client upgrades, one retainer client increased their monthly fee partly to gain access. This means SE Ranking functions both as an operational tool and as a differentiated value-add that clients visibly perceive as worth paying for. This should be maintained and, where relevant, surfaced more explicitly in retainer conversations.

---

## Billing Principles

**Preferred currency is USD.** Where a client has no strong regional preference, quote and invoice in USD. It is the most liquid and predictable currency for an international client base.

**ManyRequests dashboard clients are always billed in USD.** This is a standing rule regardless of client region.

**Other clients are billed in their regional currency** (GBP, EUR, NZD, USD) depending on where they are based. This accommodates clients who prefer to pay in their own currency and reduces friction.

**Preferred payment method is direct invoice via Xero.** Xero invoicing avoids Stripe processing fees. Stripe is used for ManyRequests dashboard billing and for clients where direct invoice is not practical.

**Stripe fee awareness:** Non-NZD payments processed through Stripe incur a 3.7% processing fee plus approximately 2% for currency conversion, approximately 5.7% total. This fee is absorbed, not passed on to clients. When routing payments, direct invoice via Xero should always be the first option to preserve margin.

---

## Revenue Concentration Risk

No single client should represent more than 25-30% of total monthly revenue. When a single client represents a disproportionate share of income, their churn creates a structural problem rather than a manageable setback.

The Physitrack churn in early 2026 (the agency's largest client at the time) is the clearest example: when a single client represented close to half of recurring revenue and they exited due to timezone friction, it reset the revenue picture significantly. The lesson is not to avoid large clients, it is to ensure that growth happens across multiple clients simultaneously, not by deepening dependence on one.

Related: clients based in significantly different time zones (UK, US) should be qualified carefully. Timezone friction is a real churn risk for an agency operating from New Zealand. It is not a dealbreaker, but it should be factored into the conversation and the delivery model. See: 04-ideal-client-profile.md.

---

## Key Metrics We Track

**Monthly recurring revenue (MRR):** Total confirmed recurring income per month in NZD equivalent. The primary health indicator.

**Monthly burn:** Total outgoing costs per month in NZD equivalent. Compared against MRR to understand margin.

**Cash buffer in months:** Current cash held divided by monthly burn. Target is six months.

**Revenue by currency:** Useful for understanding exposure to exchange rate movement and for making invoicing decisions.

**Pipeline value:** Total estimated value of leads currently in the sales pipeline. See: 09-sales-strategy.md and the HubSpot pipeline.

**Stripe fee drag:** Approximate amount lost to Stripe fees monthly. Useful for identifying which clients could be shifted to direct invoicing.

These are reviewed in finances.xlsx. Any decisions that change cost structure or revenue targets should be noted in the positional change log below.

---

## Notes

Live income, expense, and client data are tracked in finances.xlsx. All figures there are in the native currency of each transaction. When converting to NZD for reporting, use the current exchange rate at the time of review.

---

## Positional Change Log

*For internal reference. Tracks where we used to stand and where we stand now.*

| Topic | Previous position | Current position | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Financial documentation | Not formally documented | Financial philosophy, targets, and principles documented here; live data in finances.xlsx | Needed to exist in writing to support decision-making |
| Founder salaries | $52,000 NZD each per year | Target $74,000 NZD each per year; increase tied to revenue milestone | Growth-linked, not calendar-linked |
| Cash buffer | Ad-hoc | Six months of operating costs; currently ~$30,000 NZD (~2.5 months); building toward target | Structural stability, not emergency reserve |
| Billing currency preference | Varies by client | USD preferred; ManyRequests always USD; Xero direct invoice preferred over Stripe | Reduces FX risk, reduces Stripe fee drag |
| Revenue concentration awareness | Not formally considered | Active policy: no single client over 25-30% of MRR | Physitrack churn was a learning event |
| SE Ranking | Pure tool cost | Margin-generating at 3+ clients; also drives client upgrades | Changed from overhead to value-add |

pages.title: Sales Strategy
pages.content:
# 09: Sales Strategy
*Version 3.0, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: 04-ideal-client-profile.md, 07-services-and-pricing.md, 12-partnerships-and-affiliates.md*
*Note: Client referral mechanics live here. B2B partnerships live in 12.*

---

## Purpose of This Document

This document covers how Tahi Studio finds, qualifies, and closes clients. It includes the lead pipeline structure, discovery process, proposal approach, referral programme, and the things we consciously avoid. The goal is to move from a largely instinct-driven sales process to one that is consistent, trackable, and improvable over time.

---

## Sales Philosophy

We do not push. We qualify. The job of sales at Tahi is not to convert everyone who expresses interest, it is to find the people who are genuinely right for us and say yes quickly, while saying no to everyone else just as quickly. A bad fit client costs more than a lost deal.

Liam leads all sales activity. Sales is a skill being actively developed, not one assumed. That means the process should support good instincts with structure, not replace judgment with a rigid script.

See: 04-ideal-client-profile.md for a full picture of who we are and are not selling to.

---

## Lead Sources

Lead sources in rough order of current volume:

**Referrals** are the dominant channel. Existing clients, past clients, and industry contacts send work to us because they trust us. This is the healthiest lead source because the trust is pre-built and close rates are higher. Webflow partner leads are counted here, leads that come through Webflow's agency finder and partner lead generator arrive pre-qualified and have already been sent with some context. These are effectively warm referrals from a platform.

**LinkedIn** is the second channel and the one being actively built. Liam posts on technical topics, strategy, and business. Posts occasionally generate inbound interest. The StraightIn agency is running LinkedIn outreach on Liam's behalf (voice notes, AI-assisted messaging, targeted ICP). Started February 2026, 4 month contract ending June 2026. If it generates meaningful leads within the contract period it continues. If not, it does not.

**Website** generates a smaller volume of inbound leads from organic search and general brand presence. This will grow as SEO and AEO work compounds. See: 15-seo-and-aeo-strategy.md.

**Cold outreach ICP (StraightIn):** Marketers at companies with 50+ employees in New Zealand and Australia, and 100+ employees in the UK and US. Aligns with the ICP described in 04 but targets a slightly broader employee count to allow for volume.

---

## Lead Tracking, HubSpot Pipeline

All leads must be logged in HubSpot. Currently the process is informal (head and email). That needs to change.

**Pipeline stages:**

1. Inquiry, lead came in, not yet responded to
2. Contacted, responded and they are engaged
3. Discovery, discovery call happened or full email exchange complete
4. Proposal sent, proposal or quote delivered
5. Won / Lost / Stalled, outcome logged

**Properties to log on every deal:** Source (referral / Webflow / LinkedIn / website / cold), estimated value, currency, and any notes relevant to the deal context.

Logging takes less than two minutes per lead. The habit is: every first contact gets a deal created in HubSpot the same day. Everything else can be updated later. Without this data, we cannot measure close rate, identify where deals die, or evaluate whether StraightIn or any other channel is working.

**Target metrics to generate over time:** close rate by stage, close rate by source, average deal size by source, average sales cycle length.

---

## First Contact Response

When a lead reaches out (directly, via Webflow, or through any other channel), the response process is:

Acknowledge quickly. Confirm whether we can help or not. If yes, give a brief sense of who we are and what we do, then move to booking a call. If no, say so clearly and, where possible, point them in a useful direction.

The faster the response, the better. A slow reply signals disorganisation. We aim to respond to all new inquiries within the same business day.

For leads that come through the ManyRequests dashboard, billing is in USD regardless of the client's region. For all other leads, we bill in the client's regional currency but prefer USD where there is a choice. See: 08-financial-overview.md for billing currency principles.

---

## Discovery Process

Discovery happens on a call or, if the client prefers, through a structured email exchange. The goal is to understand what they need, why they need it, and whether we are the right fit.

**Core questions that come up on almost every discovery:**

What are you trying to achieve with this project? Why now, what has prompted the move? What does a successful outcome look like for you? What is your timeline? What is your budget or expected investment? Who else is involved in the decision?

**Project-specific questions that vary by context:**

What is the current site built on and what is broken about it? How many pages, and are there any complex functionality requirements? What tools do you use that need to integrate with the site? Do you have a brand established or is brand work part of this? What have you tried before and why did it not work?

The approach is repeatable core questions plus follow-the-thread. The core questions ensure consistency; the follow-up questions come from listening. We do not use a rigid script.

**Red flags in discovery to act on immediately:**

A client who cannot define what they want but expects a fixed price is not ready. We either flush out the scope with them or walk away, not both. A client suggesting a tool stack that is the wrong fit for their problem (for example, building a learning management system inside Webflow when it should not be) is flagged and redirected. If they push back, we decline. A client where budget or overall feel is off is noted and the conversation is ended gracefully. See: 04-ideal-client-profile.md for the full disqualification framework.

---

## Proposal Process

After discovery, a proposal goes out the same day where possible. Fast turnaround is a genuine competitive advantage and signals that we are organised.

**Current format:** Proposal sent via email. Structured as clear bullet points covering: project scope, what we will deliver, tools and approach, cost, and timeline. Scope and cost agreed over email is covered in our terms and conditions, no formal contract is required for smaller engagements.

**Direction of travel:** We want to move toward proposals that are more visually considered and reference relevant case studies. This does not mean complex, it means polished. A well-designed one-pager or structured email with a linked case study does more work than the same information in a plain list. See: 16-case-studies-and-proof.md when that document is populated.

**Proposal timing:** Do not delay proposals waiting for the perfect format. Speed matters more than polish right now. Build the polish gradually.

---

## Sales Cycle

A typical sales cycle from first contact to signed is seven to fourteen days. The rough sequence is: lead makes contact, response same day, call booked one to two days out, proposal sent same day as call, client signs within the week.

Sales cycles will lengthen as we move toward larger and more enterprise-level clients. Enterprise decision-making involves more stakeholders and more process. The qualification work done in discovery becomes more important at that level.

The most common deal-death points are: before a conversation happens (lead goes cold after initial contact) and after the proposal is sent (pricing, offering, or a competitor they prefer). Social proof is a factor in the latter, which is why case studies and visible work matter.

---

## Closing

We do not use pressure tactics. When the proposal is sent, we follow up once if we have not heard back within a reasonable window (three to five days). If they are not moving, we ask whether anything in the proposal needs addressing. If it is not a fit, we say so clearly and end the conversation well.

Formal contracts are used for larger engagements. Smaller projects are agreed over email, which is valid under our terms and conditions. As deal size increases, the contract process should become more considered.

---

## Project-to-Retainer Conversion

When a project is nearing completion and the working relationship has been good, we open the retainer conversation directly: we enjoyed working with you, we would like to keep working with you, and here is what that looks like.

To encourage conversion, project clients who transition to a retainer receive a 10% discount for life. This is framed as a finder's fee given to the client rather than to a referrer, it rewards the trust they built with us through the project. The discount does not expire.

This conversation should happen proactively, not as an afterthought. The right moment is roughly two to three weeks before project delivery.

See: 07-services-and-pricing.md for retainer plan structures.

---

## Client Referral Programme

We run a referral programme for existing clients. Anyone who refers a new client receives 10% of that client's contract value for life. Tracked and managed through Rewardful.

This is a meaningful incentive, it compounds over time for the referrer and generates high-quality, pre-warmed leads for us. The programme exists and is active. It needs to be communicated more consistently to clients who are likely to refer.

Tracking is automated through the Rewardful and ManyRequests/Stripe integration. When a referred client pays, the referrer's 10% is calculated and tracked automatically. No manual reconciliation required.

The referral 10% and the project-to-retainer 10% loyalty discount are separate. A referred client who also converts from project to retainer gets their own relationship managed independently.

B2B referral and partnership agreements with agencies or collaborators are documented separately. See: 12-partnerships-and-affiliates.md.

---

## Things We Consciously Avoid

We do not chase leads that have clearly gone cold more than once. We do not compete on price to win work we would not otherwise win, discounting to close a deal that is already showing budget pressure creates the wrong client relationship from day one. We do not over-promise scope during discovery to secure a yes. We do not rely on urgency or scarcity as closing tools.

---

## Things to Build

**Short term:** Get into the habit of logging every lead in HubSpot the day it arrives. This one change generates all the close rate data we currently do not have.

**Medium term:** Develop a more polished proposal format that incorporates case studies and a cleaner visual presentation. This does not need to be complex, a structured, well-branded PDF or linked one-pager would do more work than the current bullet-point email format.

**Longer term:** As volume grows through cold outreach and LinkedIn, build a lightweight follow-up sequence for leads that go quiet after first contact. This does not need to be automated to be effective.

---

## Positional Change Log

*For internal reference. Tracks where we used to stand and where we stand now.*

| Topic | Previous position | Current position | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lead tracking | All in Liam's head and email | Moving to HubSpot pipeline with 5 defined stages | Can't improve what we don't measure |
| Sales process | Instinct-driven, no documented stages | Documented stages with repeatable discovery framework | Consistency and scalability |
| Proposal format | Bullet points in email | Maintaining speed; building toward more polished format with case studies | Speed is an advantage; polish is a direction of travel |
| Cold outreach | None | StraightIn agency running LinkedIn outreach since February 2026; 4 month contract | Testing whether outbound works for our ICP |
| Referral programme | Informal and inconsistent | Active programme via Rewardful; 10% for life | Systematic version of something that was already happening informally |
| Project-to-retainer conversion | Ad-hoc conversation | Proactive conversation with 10% lifetime loyalty discount | Makes the conversion feel like a reward, not a push |

pages.title: Client Experience and Delivery
pages.content:
# 10: Client Experience & Delivery
*Version 3.0, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: 07-services-and-pricing.md, 11-tools-and-tech-stack.md*

---

## Purpose of This Document

This document covers how clients experience working with Tahi from the moment they sign to the moment a project closes or a retainer is established. It includes the onboarding process, delivery model, communication approach, revision handling, and how we move clients from one-off projects into ongoing relationships. Much of the current process is acknowledged to be informal. This document captures what exists and what we are building toward.

---

## The Client Journey, Overview

**Project clients** move through: signed agreement, onboarding, discovery and design direction, delivery, review and feedback, final handoff, and retainer conversion conversation.

**Retainer clients** move through: signed agreement, onboarding, ongoing task queue management, regular delivery, and periodic check-ins on scope and direction.

In both cases, everything client-facing runs through the ManyRequests dashboard. That is the single source of truth for tasks, requests, deliverables, communication, and billing.

---

## Onboarding

**Current state:** When a new client signs, they are added to the ManyRequests dashboard. An initial set of tasks is created based on what was discussed during discovery and the agreement. From there it is largely informal, a call or a message, and then work begins.

This is acknowledged to be underdeveloped. The goal is a structured onboarding experience that gives the client immediate confidence and clarity.

**Direction of travel:**

The first improvement is a short onboarding video made by Liam for each client. Not a generic explainer, a specific, client-facing walkthrough that covers: what their dashboard looks like, how to make requests, what to expect in terms of turnaround, and what the first few tasks are. This is recorded once per client, sent at onboarding, and lives in the dashboard. It is low effort to make and high value to receive.

The second improvement is a request library, a curated list of the types of tasks clients on a retainer plan can request. This helps clients who are unsure what to ask for, removes friction, and sets expectations about scope. The request library would be visible inside the dashboard and reflect the plan they are on. See: 07-services-and-pricing.md for plan structures.

The third improvement is a cleaner first-week experience: what happens on day one, what happens in the first week, and what the client should expect to see. Right now that structure exists in Liam's head. It needs to exist as a repeatable process.

---

## The ManyRequests Dashboard

ManyRequests is the central hub for all client-facing delivery. All task and project management, client communication, file sharing and deliverable review, billing, and request handling runs through it.

Clients on the ManyRequests dashboard are billed in USD regardless of region. This is a standing rule.

For new clients, the onboarding process begins with dashboard access, followed by a personal onboarding video (see above), and an initial task set reflecting the agreed scope.

---

## Delivery Model

Work is delivered across two track types. See: 07-services-and-pricing.md for full definitions.

**Small track** covers tasks that can be completed in a day or less, minor updates, fixes, content changes, lightweight requests. These move quickly through the queue.

**Large track** covers tasks that require more time, typically up to a week of work. These are planned and scoped more formally before starting.

For project clients, delivery is milestone-based where the project has distinct phases (discovery, design, build, review, handoff). For retainer clients, delivery is queue-based, tasks are picked up from the dashboard on an ongoing basis within the agreed monthly capacity.

For larger or higher-risk projects, Tahi works in milestones with payment tied to each stage. This protects cashflow and ensures clients remain engaged and responsive throughout. See: 07-services-and-pricing.md for how this works in practice.

---

## Communication

Client communications are handled by Liam as the primary point of contact. All communication happens through the ManyRequests dashboard rather than email, unless a client has a specific preference or an existing email thread predates dashboard setup.

Response times are kept prompt. For retainer clients, the expectation is same-day or next-business-day responses on questions and updates. For project clients, the same applies during active phases.

Clients are not left waiting without an update. If something is taking longer than expected, a brief update is sent proactively. This is a non-negotiable part of the client experience, it is one of the clearest ways to build trust without doing any additional work.

---

## Revisions and Feedback

Revisions are handled within the scope of the agreed engagement. The number of revision rounds is not always formally capped, this is something to formalise as the business grows. For now, revisions are managed pragmatically: reasonable feedback is addressed as a matter of course, and scope creep is flagged and discussed before it becomes a problem.

Feedback is requested at clear stages, end of design direction, after a major build milestone, and at final delivery. Open-ended "let us know when you have thoughts" is avoided because it invites delayed and unfocused responses.

When a client provides feedback that is vague or contradictory, we ask clarifying questions before acting on it. Acting on unclear instructions and then reworking is inefficient for both sides.

---

## Handling Difficult Clients

If a client becomes demanding beyond the scope of what was agreed, the conversation is had directly: what they are asking for is outside the agreed scope, and here is what addressing it would look like. This is not a confrontation, it is a boundary that protects both the client relationship and the quality of the work.

If a client becomes genuinely toxic (disrespectful, unreasonable, consistently operating in bad faith), the relationship is ended. This has happened. The threshold is a combination of behaviour and whether the compensation justifies the cost to the team. See: 01-brand-dna.md for the values that inform this.

---

## Project Handoff

At the end of a project, delivery includes a clean handoff: all assets transferred, relevant documentation provided, and the client able to operate independently if they choose to. We do not create intentional dependency or withhold information at the end of an engagement.

The handoff conversation is also the opening for the retainer discussion. See: 09-sales-strategy.md (Project-to-Retainer Conversion) for how that conversation works.

---

## Testimonials and Case Studies

Asking for testimonials is uncomfortable for most people. The discomfort comes from feeling like you are imposing or appearing needy. The fix is timing, framing, and making it easy.

**Timing:** Do not ask at handoff when the client is in transition mode and thinking about what comes next. Ask two to four weeks after a successful delivery or a strong milestone, when they have had time to see the result and still feel good about it. For retainer clients, a positive moment mid-engagement can be the right time.

**Framing:** Ask as a genuine favour, not as a formal request. Keep it short and give them a clear out.

**Making it easy:** Offer two options every time. First, send three short questions they can reply to in a few sentences. Second, offer to write a draft yourself based on what you worked on together and have them approve or edit it. The draft option converts significantly better because it removes almost all effort on their side.

**Format options:** A written testimonial (one to three sentences) is the minimum ask and most people will agree to it. A short video testimonial (under a minute) is more persuasive when used as social proof but takes more from the client. For larger or more distinctive projects, a proper case study (problem, solution, outcome, quote) is worth pursuing.

The ask should feel like a casual email, not a form or a process. See: 09-sales-strategy.md for the testimonial ask email template.

Case study content, once gathered, lives in 16-case-studies-and-proof.md.

---

## Offboarding

If a retainer client ends their engagement, the process is: acknowledge the decision, agree an end date, complete any work in progress, deliver a final handoff, and close out billing cleanly. No pressure, no guilt, no drawn-out goodbye.

We aim for clients to leave feeling like they were treated well even if they do not return. Many do return, or refer others. The quality of the offboarding directly affects both.

---

## Things to Build

**Short term:** Onboarding video for each new client. Recorded by Liam, specific to the client's context, delivered at the point of dashboard setup. This is the highest-leverage near-term improvement to the client experience.

**Medium term:** Request library inside the ManyRequests dashboard, organised by plan type. Helps retainer clients understand what they can ask for and reduces the friction of getting started.

**Longer term:** A formalised first-week checklist and onboarding sequence so that the quality of the first impression does not depend on Liam remembering to do things in the right order.

---

## Positional Change Log

*For internal reference. Tracks where we used to stand and where we stand now.*

| Topic | Previous position | Current position | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Onboarding | Informal, add to dashboard and start | Acknowledged gap; building toward video onboarding, request library, and structured first week | Client confidence is built in the first 48 hours |
| Client communication | Mix of email and dashboard | Dashboard-first for all clients; email only where necessary | Single source of truth, cleaner record |
| Delivery model | Informal, no defined track structure | Small track (up to a day) and large track (up to a week) as per 07-services-and-pricing.md | Consistent framing for clients and internally |
| Revision handling | Ad-hoc | Pragmatic but flagging scope creep early; formalising in future | Prevent rework from unclear feedback |
| Difficult clients | Managed individually | Direct conversation first; exit if behaviour is genuinely problematic | Protects team and sets a standard |
| Project handoff | Implicit | Clean handoff as standard; also the trigger for retainer conversation | Ends engagements well and opens the next one |
| Testimonial asks | Ad-hoc and avoided | Structured approach: timing, framing, two easy options (questions or draft for approval) | Removing the discomfort removes the delay |

pages.title: Tools and Tech Stack
pages.content:
# 11: Tools and Tech Stack
*Version 3.4, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: 08-financial-overview.md, 10-client-experience-and-delivery.md, 09-sales-strategy.md*
*Note: Tool costs are tracked live in the Finances Expenses spreadsheet. Do not duplicate figures here.*

---

## What This Document Is

A complete reference for every tool the business runs on, what it does, and why it is here. Also covers the technical capabilities the team brings: what we can build, and the distinction between what we advertise and what we simply know how to do.

---

## How We Think About the Stack

The default position on any new tool is no. A tool earns its place by solving a real problem that is not already solved elsewhere in the stack. Integration matters: tools that talk to each other via Zapier or native connections are preferred over isolated ones that create manual work.

The stack is not a trophy cabinet. Knowing how to use something does not mean it belongs in the business. The same applies to technical capabilities: the full range of what Liam can build is much wider than what Tahi actively sells or promotes. Advertising every capability dilutes the positioning. We lead with what we do best at scale.

---

## Business Operations Stack

### Summary Table

| Tool | Function | Billing |
|---|---|---|
| Webflow | Website and web app build platform | Yearly |
| Relume | Webflow component and wireframe library | Subscription |
| Figma | Design, prototyping, wireframing, FigJam, Make | Yearly, 2 seats |
| Canva | Quick graphics, social assets | Subscription |
| Midjourney + AI generation tools | AI image and video generation for client and internal work | Subscription |
| ManyRequests | Client portal, request management, subscription billing, file delivery | Yearly |
| HubSpot | CRM, sales pipeline (free tier) | Free |
| Xero | Accounting, invoicing, payroll | Monthly |
| Stripe | Payment processing (linked to ManyRequests and Rewardful) | Per transaction |
| Google Workspace | Gmail, Drive, Docs, Sheets, Meet, Calendar, booking pages | Monthly |
| Slack | Internal team communication and client messaging | Yearly |
| Loom | Async video walkthroughs, screen recording, client communication | Yearly |
| MailerLite | Email marketing, contact list | Monthly |
| SE Ranking | SEO and AEO tracking for Tahi and clients | Monthly |
| Rewardful | Referral and affiliate programme (integrated with Stripe) | Monthly |
| Zapier | Automation between tools | Monthly |
| Hostinger | Domain registrar for tahi.studio | Yearly |
| Cloudflare | DNS management for tahi.studio | Free tier |
| Matomo | Privacy-first website analytics | Monthly |
| Vidzflow | Video hosting | Monthly |
| Claude (Anthropic) | AI assistant, used across writing, ops, and product development | Monthly |
| Gemini (Google) | AI assistant, secondary | Subscription |
| Antigravity | Coding tool | Subscription |
| LinkedIn Sales Navigator | LinkedIn prospecting (required by StraightIn contract) | Monthly |
| Internal task dashboard | Custom task management dashboard | Internal |

---

### Notes on Key Tools

**Webflow** is the primary build platform for all client work. Liam's Webflow expertise runs deep: complex animations, CMS, membership, ecommerce, and API integrations. The Tahi Studio site and the Play Therapy site (hosted for free as a personal arrangement) both run on Webflow. See 08-financial-overview.md for the workspace and hosting cost structure.

**Hostinger and Cloudflare** handle the domain infrastructure. The tahi.studio domain is registered with Hostinger. DNS is managed through Cloudflare: the domain's nameservers point to Cloudflare, which handles all DNS records including routing to Webflow's servers. Cloudflare sits in front of the site and provides an additional layer of performance and security.

**ManyRequests** is the operational core. All client requests come in here, all file delivery goes out here, and subscription billing runs through it via Stripe. It is the single place clients go to interact with us. See 10-client-experience-and-delivery.md for how the dashboard fits into the client journey.

**Figma** is the full design environment. Staci uses the complete suite: design files, FigJam for workshops and planning, and Figma Make for prototyping. All design work before it reaches Webflow lives here.

**SE Ranking** is worth noting because it generates revenue, not just costs. We charge $150 USD per client per month for SEO/AEO monitoring and reporting. The tool costs roughly £164 per month total. At three or more clients using it, it is margin-positive. See 08-financial-overview.md for the full billing model.

**HubSpot** is on the free tier. It runs a five-stage pipeline: Inquiry, Contacted, Discovery, Proposal Sent, Won/Lost/Stalled. No CRM features beyond what the free tier provides are currently needed. See 09-sales-strategy.md for how the pipeline is used.

**MailerLite** holds the marketing contact list. For the current subscriber count, check the MailerLite MCP directly. Around 4,000 as of March 2026. This is a significant asset. The list is not heavily monetised yet. It is the primary channel for any future email marketing, nurture sequences, or product launches.

**Rewardful** handles referral tracking automatically. It integrates directly with Stripe, so when a referred client pays, the referrer's 10% is tracked without manual work. See 09-sales-strategy.md for how the referral programme works.

**Zapier** connects the stack. The primary active automation is blog-post-to-LinkedIn: when a post publishes, Zapier generates a LinkedIn post in Tahi's brand voice and schedules it. Other automations are added as the need arises.

**Claude and Gemini** are used regularly across writing, operations, research, and product work. Claude is the primary AI tool.

---

## Contractors and Agencies

**Beany** is the external accountant. Monthly engagement. Works alongside Xero.

**StraightIn** is a LinkedIn outreach agency running a four-month contract from February to June 2026. They manage outreach from Liam's LinkedIn profile. LinkedIn Sales Navigator is billed separately because StraightIn requires it. The contract will be reviewed at the end of the term based on leads generated. See 09-sales-strategy.md for context on where LinkedIn sits in the lead source mix.

---

## Technical Capabilities

This section documents what the team can build. It is an internal reference. It is not a complete list of what we advertise.

### What We Lead With

These are the capabilities we actively sell and deliver at a high level of proficiency.

**Webflow:** The core. Design, development, CMS, animations, ecommerce, membership, accessibility. This is where the depth is.

**HTML, CSS, JavaScript, TypeScript:** The foundational web stack. Clean, hand-coded implementations where Webflow alone is not sufficient.

**No-code and low-code data integration:** Wized and Xano for connecting Webflow front ends to real data layers. Airtable as a lightweight database for client projects requiring dynamic content, user accounts, or backend logic without a full custom build.

**Figma:** End-to-end design. UI, UX, wireframing, prototyping. Staci leads all design work.

---

### Webflow Ecosystem

These are the libraries, frameworks, and tools we use within Webflow projects. Knowing these is part of building Webflow sites properly, not an add-on.

**Client First:** The CSS class naming convention used on all Webflow builds. Keeps projects consistent, readable, and maintainable by any developer who inherits them.

**Finsweet:** The go-to library for Webflow CMS functionality that Webflow does not natively support, including CMS filtering, load more, nested CMS, and form enhancements.

**Lumos:** Used for Webflow-specific utilities and enhancements where relevant.

**Relume:** Component and wireframe library for Webflow. Speeds up scoping and early-stage layout work. Included in the operations stack.

**Lottie Files:** JSON-based animation integration. Used for lightweight, scalable animations embedded in Webflow builds without video overhead.

**Spline:** 3D design and animation tool. Used for interactive 3D elements embedded in Webflow where the project warrants it.

**Rive:** Interactive animation runtime. Used for state-based animations, looping graphics, and interactive UI elements in Webflow.

**Native Webflow features used beyond the builder:** Webflow Ecommerce for product and checkout builds. Webflow Localization for multilingual sites natively. Webflow Optimize for A/B testing and experimentation. Webflow Analyze for built-in site analytics. These are platform-native and do not require a separate tool subscription in most cases.

**Forms:** Native Webflow forms for standard use cases. Typeform for more complex or conversational forms. Multistep forms built with Formly or custom JavaScript where the native Webflow form is insufficient.

---

### Client Integration Capabilities

When a client project requires connecting Webflow to an external platform or service, these are the integrations we have experience with. This is a reference list, not a sales catalogue. We scope each project to determine which integrations are appropriate.

**Membership and user accounts:** Memberstack, Outseta. Both provide user authentication, gated content, and member portals layered on top of Webflow.

**Ecommerce:** Webflow native ecommerce for straightforward product and checkout builds. Shopify via Shopyflow for clients already on Shopify who want a Webflow front end.

**Automation and workflow:** Zapier, Make (formerly Integromat), N8n. Zapier is in the operations stack. Make and N8n are available for projects requiring more complex automation logic or self-hosted workflows.

**Events and ticketing:** Eventbrite, Ticket Tailor. Integrated into Webflow builds for event listing, registration, and ticketing.

**CRM and email platform integration:** HubSpot, Salesforce, ActiveCampaign, Mailchimp, MailerLite. Form submissions, lead capture, and contact list syncing from Webflow into whatever CRM or email platform the client runs.

**Analytics and behaviour tracking:** Matomo (in the operations stack), Google Analytics, Google Tag Manager. Hotjar and Microsoft Clarity for heatmaps and session recording on client sites where behaviour tracking is part of the scope.

**Translation and localisation:** Weglot for adding a translation layer to existing Webflow sites. Webflow's native Localization feature for projects where multilingual is built in from the start.

**Search and filtering:** Jetboost for Webflow site search and advanced filtering beyond what Finsweet covers.

**Video hosting:** Vidzflow (in the operations stack), Vimeo, and Wistia for client projects requiring hosted video with more control over player behaviour and analytics than a standard embed.

**Cloud infrastructure:** AWS and Google Cloud. Available for projects requiring hosted backend services, storage, or serverless functions beyond what Webflow and standard no-code tools provide.

**AI tools used in builds:** ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini. Used in development and content workflows. Not client-facing integrations by default, but available.

---

### Own Products

**Nodeo** is Tahi's own SaaS product, currently in late beta and approaching its first public launch. It turns any Webflow form into a progressive two-step adaptive form: each visitor sees a curated subset of the full question list based on what they have already answered. Email is always step one. Subsequent visits build the complete profile across two or three returns instead of one wall-of-questions submission.

Hosted at getnodeo.com. Backend on AWS (eu-central-1, encrypted at rest), dashboard on Webflow Cloud. Auth is Clerk; billing is Stripe. End-user data is hashed and EU-resident.

See the dedicated Nodeo doc for product detail, pricing, target user, GDPR posture, and the roadmap to launch.

---

### What We Can Do but Do Not Actively Advertise

Liam has working proficiency in the following. These are available for the right project but are not part of the standard positioning.

**React and Node.js:** For projects that genuinely require a JavaScript framework or a server-side runtime. Not the default recommendation.

**Astro:** Static site framework. Useful for content-heavy sites where Webflow is not the right fit.

**PHP and MySQL:** Legacy web development. Available but not actively sought.

**Python:** Scripting, automation, and data work. Used internally more than for client projects.

**Serverless and backend infrastructure:** AWS Lambda and similar. Available for projects that require it.

**AI image and video generation:** Midjourney and other generation tools are used internally and can be applied to client creative work where appropriate.

---

### What We Do Not Offer

We do not build native mobile apps. We do not offer DevOps or infrastructure management as a service. We do not take on projects where the primary requirement is a stack we do not work in by default, such as full custom React SPAs or e-commerce on platforms other than Webflow.

---

## Tools We Have Moved Away From

<!-- Add when relevant. Note what the tool was, what replaced it, and why. -->

---

## Tools We Are Evaluating

<!-- Anything on the radar but not yet adopted. -->

---

## Notes

<!-- POSITIONAL CHANGE LOG -->

Notes on SE Ranking billing model, ManyRequests, HubSpot pipeline, MailerLite list size, Rewardful automation, and Zapier. Contractors section covering Beany and StraightIn. Technical capabilities section added with primary capabilities, secondary capabilities, and explicit list of what we do not offer. -->
<!-- v3.1, March 2026: Webflow ecosystem section added (Client First, Finsweet, Lumos, Relume, Lottie, Spline, Rive). Client integration capabilities section added (Memberstack, Outseta, Zapier/Make/N8n, Eventbrite/Ticket Tailor, CRM and email platforms, AWS/Google Cloud). Nodeo noted as own product in development. -->
<!-- v3.2, March 2026: Google Workspace updated to include booking pages (Google Calendar). Webflow ecosystem expanded: native Webflow features (Ecommerce, Localization, Optimize, Analyze), Typeform, Formly/multistep forms. Client integrations expanded: Shopify via Shopyflow, Hotjar/Microsoft Clarity, Weglot, Webflow Localization, Jetboost, Vimeo/Wistia video hosting. -->
<!-- v3.3, March 2026: MailerLite subscriber count removed from static fields. Reference updated to MailerLite MCP as live source of truth, with "around 4,000 as of March 2026" as a directional figure only. -->

# 12: Partnerships and Affiliates
*Version 1.1, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: 09-sales-strategy.md, 11-tools-and-tech-stack.md, 08-financial-overview.md*

---

## What This Document Is

A reference for every formal partnership, affiliate arrangement, and key referral relationship the business holds. Covers status, what each arrangement means practically, and obligations or renewal requirements where relevant.

---

## Webflow

### Premium Partner Status

Tahi Studio is a Webflow Premium Partner, one of six Premium Partners in New Zealand as of March 2026. Certification is valid until 1 February 2027.

The full benefits breakdown across tiers (Onboarding, Certified, Premium) is below for reference. Tahi holds Premium status.

**Revenue share:**

| Benefit | Onboarding | Certified | Premium |
|---|---|---|---|
| Site plans and add-ons | 20% | 20% | 20% |
| Enterprise plans | n/a | 10% | 10% |

**Product experience:**

| Benefit | Onboarding | Certified | Premium |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access to Partner Hub | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Early beta access | No | Yes | Yes |
| Partner Sandbox access | No | Yes | Yes |

**Community:**

| Benefit | Onboarding | Certified | Premium |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partner Community and Slack | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Partner newsletter | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Partner exclusive events | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Partner advisory board | No | No | Yes |
| Partner summit | No | No | Yes |

**Marketplace:**

| Benefit | Onboarding | Certified | Premium |
|---|---|---|---|
| Public partner profile | No | Yes | Yes |
| Partner Directory listing | No | Listed in Directory | Preferential placement |
| Matchmaking | No | Included in Matchmaking | Preferred status in Matchmaking |

**Support:**

| Benefit | Onboarding | Certified | Premium |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partner office hours | No | Yes | Yes |
| Prioritised customer support | No | Yes | Yes |
| Direct point of contact at Webflow | No | No | Yes |
| Sales Engineering and Technical Architect support | No | No | Yes |

**Enablement and marketing:**

| Benefit | Onboarding | Certified | Premium |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partner Badges asset kit | No | Yes | Yes |
| Access to enablement materials | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Partner training and education | No | Yes | Yes |
| Editorial opportunities | No | Yes | Yes |
| Eligible for annual Webflow Partner Awards | No | Yes | Yes |
| Nomination for Industry Awards | No | No | Yes |
| Co-marketing and co-selling opportunities | No | No | Yes |

The revenue share applies when clients purchase or renew qualifying Webflow plans through Tahi's partner link. See 08-financial-overview.md for how this income is tracked.

---

### Maintaining Premium Status: Points System

Premium status requires 4,000 points per year. Points are earned across three categories: site plans sold to clients, add-ons, and community contributions.

**Site plans:**

| Plan | First purchase | Renewal |
|---|---|---|
| Basic | 100 | 50 |
| CMS | 150 | 75 |
| Business | 250 | 150 |
| Ecommerce Standard | 250 | 150 |
| Ecommerce Plus | 500 | 300 |
| Ecommerce Advanced | 1,500 | 750 |
| Enterprise | Custom, based on contract value | n/a |

**Add-ons:**

| Add-on | Points |
|---|---|
| Localization Essential | 50 per locale |
| Localization Advanced | 200 per locale |
| CMS Items (5,000 item pack) | 200 |
| Bandwidth (50 GB extra) | 150 |
| Optimize (25k page views/mo) | 1,500 |
| Optimize (50k page views/mo) | 2,000 |
| Optimize (100k page views/mo) | 3,000 |
| Optimize (250k page views/mo) | 4,000 |
| Optimize (500k page views/mo) | 5,000 |
| Analyze (2k sessions/mo) | 50 |
| Analyze (5k sessions/mo) | 125 |
| Analyze (10k sessions/mo) | 200 |
| Analyze (25k sessions/mo) | 300 |
| Analyze (50k sessions/mo) | 500 |
| Analyze (100k sessions/mo) | 1,000 |
| Analyze (250k sessions/mo) | 1,250 |
| Analyze (500k sessions/mo) | 1,500 |

**Community contributions:**

| Contribution | Points |
|---|---|
| Major event (Webflow Conf, Partner Summit) | 500 |
| Standard event (partner webinars, workshops) | 250 |
| Co-created content with Webflow team | 350 per piece |
| Awwwards: Site of the Day | 500 |
| Awwwards: Site of the Month | 1,000 |
| Awwwards: Site of the Year | 1,500 |
| Awwwards: Honourable Mention | 100 |

Note: design recognition points require submitting the Awwwards form to Webflow after a win. Some add-on categories are capped at 1,500 points per year.

---

### Webflow Global Leaders (Individual)

Both Liam and Staci hold Webflow Global Leader status as individuals, separate from the company's Premium Partner certification. This designation provides:

- Access to the Webflow sales dashboard for upselling, optimising, and analysing Webflow Enterprise opportunities
- Early access to beta features and upcoming product releases
- A seat on the Partner Advisory Board
- Invitations to Partner Summits
- Priority support channel
- Direct relationships with Webflow's engineering team and senior staff

Global Leader status is individual, not company-level. It reflects personal standing within the Webflow partner ecosystem and feeds directly into the depth of Tahi's Webflow knowledge and advisory capability.

---

## Optibase

Tahi is an Optibase partner. Optibase is an A/B testing and conversion rate optimisation tool built specifically for Webflow. The partnership gives access to the tool and positions Tahi to offer CRO testing as part of client engagements where experimentation is in scope. See 11-tools-and-tech-stack.md for where this fits in the technical capabilities.

---

## Hello Clever (In Progress)

Hello Clever is an Australian-based payment processor that handles international payments. Tahi is in the process of formalising a partnership. As part of this, Tahi is building a Webflow app to integrate Hello Clever's payment processing directly into Webflow sites.

Once the partnership is confirmed and the app is live, Tahi expects to receive a percentage of revenue generated through the integration. This will be documented here when the split is agreed.

The partnership is ongoing and this entry will be updated once it is confirmed.

---

## Figma (In Progress)

Tahi is working towards becoming a Figma partner. This is in progress. Details to be added when the arrangement is confirmed.

---

## Tahi Affiliate Programme

Tahi runs its own referral programme through Rewardful. Anyone who refers a client receives 10% of the project or subscription value for the lifetime of that client's engagement.

The programme is tracked automatically via Rewardful's integration with Stripe. No manual intervention is required once a referrer is registered. See 09-sales-strategy.md for how the programme is positioned and 11-tools-and-tech-stack.md for the Rewardful and Stripe setup.

---

## heyWebTeam (White Label)

heyWebTeam is Tahi's white label development partner for work that falls outside Webflow. When a client project requires WordPress, custom application development, bespoke data projects, or other non-Webflow software, heyWebTeam delivers under the Tahi engagement.

The arrangement means Tahi can take on a broader scope of client work without turning projects away. heyWebTeam operates under Tahi's brand and client relationship for the duration of any engagement that involves them.

This is not a formal joint venture. It is a trusted white label arrangement used on a project-by-project basis when the scope requires it.

---

## Dante Media

Dante Media is a past Tahi client that now includes Tahi in their own proposals. They handle branding, marketing, and positioning. When a project requires a website component, they bring Tahi in for that scope.

This is an informal but active arrangement. It is collaborative rather than referral-based: both businesses are named in proposals when the scope calls for it. Tahi does not pay or receive a referral fee in this arrangement.

---

## Engaging Data

Engaging Data is a data consultancy Tahi refers clients to when a project requires dedicated data work beyond Tahi's scope. They refer clients back to Tahi in the same way. Contact is Jack Herbert.

This is an informal mutual referral arrangement with no formal agreement in place.

---

## Spot Digital

Spot Digital is a solo freelancer with a formal referral arrangement in place. Work is referred between the two parties when a project falls outside one party's current capacity or focus. This is a peer relationship rather than a structured partnership.

---

## Informal Referral Network

Beyond the documented arrangements above, Tahi receives leads from a broader informal network of contacts, past clients, and collaborators. These relationships are not governed by formal agreements. They are maintained through good work, clear follow-through, and staying visible in relevant circles.

Where an informal referral converts to a paid engagement, the referring party is eligible to be enrolled in the Tahi affiliate programme if appropriate.

---

## Notes
