Hello Clever updated with revenue split detail. Added Dante Media (collaborative proposals, branding and marketing). Added Engaging Data (informal mutual referral, contact Jack Herbert). -->
<!-- v1.0, March 2026: First version. Webflow Premium Partner status documented with full points structure and benefits tables. Webflow Global Leaders section (Liam and Staci individually). Optibase partnership. Hello Clever (in progress). Figma (in progress). Tahi affiliate programme cross-referenced to 09 and 11. heyWebTeam white label arrangement. Spot Digital referral contact. Informal referral network. -->

# 13: Marketing Strategy
*Version 1.0, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: 01-brand-dna.md, 04-ideal-client-profile.md, 09-sales-strategy.md, 12-partnerships-and-affiliates.md, 14-content-guidelines.md, 15-seo-and-aeo-strategy.md*

---

## What This Document Is

An overview of how Tahi attracts attention, grows its audience, and generates leads. Covers active channels, the email list, lead magnets, outbound, and what is planned but not yet running. Content execution detail lives in 14-content-guidelines.md. SEO and AEO strategy lives in 15-seo-and-aeo-strategy.md.

---

## Audience Segments

Tahi has two distinct audiences that require different treatment. Mixing them is a risk to both.

**Freelancers and agency owners.** The majority of the MailerLite list. These people found Tahi through the Project Calculator tool (see Lead Magnets below). They are not Tahi clients and are unlikely to become them. They are a strong audience for templates, any future course or educational product, and Nodeo. They are also good affiliate programme candidates who can refer their own clients. Do not market agency services to this segment.

**Potential clients.** Businesses that need a Webflow website, redesign, or ongoing retainer. These reach Tahi through the partner and expert directories, StraightIn outreach, the Tahi website, referrals, and Liam's personal LinkedIn. Client leads are tagged in MailerLite with the "Client - Status" custom field, which separates them from the freelancer audience in campaigns.

---

## Email Marketing

**Platform:** MailerLite. See 11-tools-and-tech-stack.md for billing and plan details.

**List size:** 4,033 subscribers as of March 2026.

**Subscriber origin:** The majority of the list came in through the Project Calculator for freelancers (originally under the CashYourFlow brand, migrated to Tahi Studio in September 2025). A smaller portion are potential client leads and Webflow contacts.

**Custom fields in use:** The list captures meaningful data at sign-up. Freelancer and agency subscribers have F&A fields recorded (core service, experience level, hourly rate, skills and tech, estimated project quote). Client leads have Client fields recorded (status, service need, project type, work preference, estimated project cost, integrations). UTM fields capture source, medium, and campaign for attribution. Nodeo User is flagged separately.

**Active automations:**

| Automation | Trigger | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Send Template | Joins group | Delivers a template to the subscriber on download |
| 2025 Pricing Wrapped (With Calc CTA) | Joins group | Delivers the Webflow Pricing Wrapped with a project calculator CTA |
| 2025 Pricing Wrapped (Without Calc CTA) | Joins group | Delivers the Webflow Pricing Wrapped without the CTA |
| Project Calculator: Welcome Sequence | Joins group | Four-email nurture sequence for new Project Calculator subscribers. Delivers pricing, pushback handling, retainer pitching, and affiliate programme over 16 days. Live as of March 2026. |

**Welcome sequence — what each email covers:**

- Email 1, day 2 — "You're Probably Undercharging": Liam intro, why he built the calculator, hooks on the gap between what freelancers charge and what projects actually cost. Goal: establish credibility and earn the next open.
- Email 2, day 5 — "What to Say When a Client Says It's Too Expensive": how to handle price pushback without discounting. Value-anchoring before the proposal goes out. Goal: give the reader something immediately usable.
- Email 3, day 10 — "Why Retainers Beat Projects Every Time": the case for predictable income over project cycles, and how to pitch a retainer at project end. Goal: shift how they think about client relationships.
- Email 4, day 16 — "On Working Together": the Tahi affiliate/referral programme as a bidirectional relationship, plus an invitation to connect on Liam's LinkedIn. Goal: introduce the programme and open an ongoing channel.

MailerLite automation ID: 181531342084768840. Written in Mode 1 (Liam as Tahi Author). See 02b-liam-personal-voice.md for voice guidance.

**Recent campaigns:**

| Campaign | Sent | Open rate | Click rate | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Webflow Pricing Wrapped 2025 | December 2025 | 28.7% | 2.83% | Sent to full list excluding existing clients |
| CashYourFlow Migration | September 2025 | 38.96% | 6.3% | Migration email moving CashYourFlow subscribers to Tahi Studio |

**How the list is used:** Campaign-based. Sends go out when there is something worth sending. No fixed newsletter cadence at present, though a regular cadence is a goal. The list is not used to pitch agency services to the freelancer segment.

**What the list could be used for** (audience is predominantly freelancers and small agencies):

- Template launches and sales — the most direct commercial use
- Nodeo launch and waitlist nurture
- Affiliate programme recruitment — these people know other freelancers with clients
- Regular newsletter for the freelancer/agency audience
- New lead magnet releases and announcements
- Re-engagement campaigns before major sends to keep the list healthy
- A course or educational product launch when that becomes relevant

**Segmentation approach:** Existing clients are excluded from public-facing campaigns using the Client - Status field filter. Freelancer and client segments can be separated via Contact Type or the presence of F&A versus Client fields.

---

## Content Marketing

**Target cadence:** 2 blog posts per week published on the Tahi website, with 2 LinkedIn posts per week derived from those same posts. This was the previous output level and is the target to return to. Content execution rules live in 14-content-guidelines.md. SEO targeting and keyword approach live in 15-seo-and-aeo-strategy.md.

**Blog to LinkedIn workflow:** Blog posts are published first, then adapted for LinkedIn. The Zapier automation pushes new blog content to LinkedIn automatically, though final posts may be reviewed or written from scratch for better native formatting. See 11-tools-and-tech-stack.md for the Zapier setup.

**LinkedIn (personal):** Liam posts on his personal LinkedIn profile to promote Tahi, share work, and build visibility with the right audience. This is separate from any company page presence and carries more reach and trust than a brand account alone.

**YouTube:** Planned under the Tahi brand. Timing is not confirmed. When it launches, the channel will be for the freelancer and agency audience rather than client prospects, which aligns with the rest of the content direction.

**Templates:** Tahi is building a template library. Zero templates are live as of March 2026, with two at approximately 95% completion. Template releases will anchor content and campaign sends. The freelancer audience on the email list is the primary market for paid templates.

---

## Lead Magnets and Assets

**Project Calculator for freelancers.** The primary list growth tool. Freelancers and agency owners use the calculator on the Tahi website to estimate project costs. On completion they enter their email to receive the result, which adds them to the MailerLite list with their calculator data recorded in the F&A custom fields. This is the source of most of the 4,033 subscribers.

**Tahi Studio Wrapped (Webflow Pricing Wrapped 2025).** An interactive breakdown of Webflow pricing for 2025, delivered by email. The campaign had two automation versions: one with a project calculator CTA and one without. 3,704 sends, 28.7% open rate. This asset lives on the Tahi website and in Webflow. It is a strong piece of shareable content and a proof of both brand quality and Webflow product knowledge.

---

## Outbound

**StraightIn.** LinkedIn outreach running from February to June 2026, operating through Liam's LinkedIn profile. The campaign is a 50/50 mix of cold direct messages and AI-generated voice notes, both aimed at booking calls and generating leads. See 11-tools-and-tech-stack.md for the StraightIn setup and 09-sales-strategy.md for how these leads are handled once contact is made.

---

## Inbound Directories

**Webflow Partner Directory.** Tahi's Premium Partner status gives preferential placement in the directory and preferred status in Webflow's matchmaking. This generates a consistent stream of inbound leads from businesses that come to Webflow's own site looking for a partner. See 12-partnerships-and-affiliates.md for full partner status detail.

**Finsweet Client First Experts Directory.** Tahi is listed as a certified expert in the Finsweet Client First directory, which is a niche but relevant source of inbound leads from clients who specifically value Client First methodology. Leads from this source are occasional but pre-qualified by definition: anyone searching a Client First directory already understands what they are looking for.

---

## Community

**Webflow Local Community Event.** Liam and Staci are working to establish a local Webflow community event in their area, bringing Webflow practitioners together in person. This is not a direct lead generation play and the attendees are not the primary client ICP. It is a community contribution and a way to stay visible and connected in the local Webflow ecosystem.

---

## What We Do Not Do

No paid advertising. No speaking engagements currently. No cold email.

---

## Notes

<!-- POSITIONAL CHANGE LOG -->
<!-- v1.0, March 2026: First version. Covers two audience segments (freelancers/agencies and client leads), full MailerLite overview (4,033 subscribers, custom fields, automations, recent campaigns), content cadence targets (2 blog + 2 LinkedIn per week), templates in progress, Project Calculator and Pricing Wrapped as lead magnets, StraightIn outbound, Webflow and Finsweet directories, community event in progress. -->

pages.title: Content Guidelines
pages.content:
# 14 — Content Guidelines
*Version 1.0, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: 02-tone-of-voice.md, 02b-liam-personal-voice.md, 02c-staci-personal-voice.md, 13-marketing-strategy.md, 15-seo-and-aeo-strategy.md*

---

## What This Document Is

How we show up across every content channel: what we publish, how often, and what the content is for. Voice and format rules are not repeated here. For those, see 02-tone-of-voice.md. For strategy, audience segmentation, and email marketing detail, see 13-marketing-strategy.md.

---

## LinkedIn (Company Page)

**Who owns it:** Tahi brand voice. Managed by Liam or Staci. Not attributed to a specific founder.

**Cadence:** 2 posts per week, derived from blog posts published that week.

**Content types:**
- Blog-derived posts (primary): auto-generated via Zapier when a blog post publishes. See 02 LinkedIn Auto-Generated section for format rules.
- Showcase posts: case study results, project launches, or notable client work. Roughly one every two weeks.
- Hashtags: 4-5 per post, always #TahiStudio. Company page only.

**Format and voice:** See 02-tone-of-voice.md → LinkedIn, Auto-Generated Posts via Zapier.

---

## LinkedIn (Personal — Liam)

**Who owns it:** Liam. Personal voice, not Tahi brand.

**Cadence:** Target 3 posts per week. Mix of blog-derived and original short-form.

**Content types:**
- Technical observations: a Webflow behaviour, a development pattern, something Liam noticed doing client work.
- Business and process insights: how projects work, how to run a client relationship, what actually drives results.
- Blog-derived posts: adapted from Tahi blog posts for native LinkedIn format, not copied directly.
- Occasional personal takes: dry observations, things worth saying that do not fit a formal blog post.

**Format and voice:** See 02b-liam-personal-voice.md for the full Mode 2 playbook. No hashtags. No em/en dashes.

---

## LinkedIn (Personal — Staci)

**Who owns it:** Staci. Personal voice, not Tahi brand.

**Cadence:** No fixed target defined yet. Post when there is something worth saying.

**Content types:**
- Design observations: how design makes people feel, what makes something work visually.
- Process and craft: the decisions behind a design, what gets removed and why.
- Personal takes: connecting design to life, relatable moments from the work.

**Format and voice:** See 02c-staci-personal-voice.md for the full Mode 2 playbook. No hashtags. 50-80 words per post. No em/en dashes.

---

## Blog / Long-Form Content

**Who authors it:** Liam for technical and business topics. Staci for design, brand, UX, and human-side topics. All published under the Tahi brand with the author credited.

**Cadence:** 2 posts per week. This was the previous output level and is the target to return to.

**Content types:**
- Tutorials and how-tos: step-by-step guidance on Webflow, design, or web strategy. Most common format.
- Opinion and positioning: a clear take on something in the industry. Must have a point of view, not just a summary.
- Case study adjacent: a piece inspired by a client result or problem, without identifying the client unless they have approved it.
- Code snippets: short, publishable techniques. See growth-priorities-2026.md item 4 for the code snippet strategy.

**Format and voice:** See 02-tone-of-voice.md → Blog Posts (Content Mode). 800-1,500 words. E-E-A-T principles. Hook, body, low-friction CTA at close.

**SEO integration:** See 15-seo-and-aeo-strategy.md for keyword targeting and heading guidance. Write for the reader first, then check against the keyword list.

**Workflow:** Blog post published first. Zapier auto-generates the company LinkedIn post. Liam may adapt a personal LinkedIn post separately.

---

## Email

**Platform:** MailerLite. Two distinct audiences (freelancers/agency owners and client leads). Do not mix them. Full segmentation and automation detail in 13-marketing-strategy.md.

**Active automations:** Four running as of March 2026. See 13 for the full list and welcome sequence detail.

**Newsletter (goal, not yet running):** Monthly send to the freelancer and agency audience. Personal note from Liam: one thing noticed or learned this month, plus one or two useful items. Under 400 words. Not a blog digest. See growth-priorities-2026.md item 2 for the full brief.

**Campaign sends:** When there is something worth sending. Template launches, Nodeo updates, major new content, re-engagement. Not on a forced cadence.

**Format and voice:** See 02-tone-of-voice.md → Email Scenarios for worked examples. See 13-marketing-strategy.md for automation setup and list management.

---

## Web Copy

**Who authors it:** Liam and Staci together. Staci leads structure and layout thinking. Liam leads persuasion and conversion copy.

**What it is for:** Attracting, convincing, and converting potential clients. Not the freelancer audience.

**Format and voice:** See 02-tone-of-voice.md → Website Copy (Marketing Mode) and Marketing Mode for the full framework. StoryBrand structure: client as hero, Tahi as guide, clear path forward.

**Copy source of truth:** When website copy is written, it lives in the 18-series docs (18a through 18e). Those docs are the single source of truth for page copy, not this one.

---

## Instagram

Not currently active. Not a priority. To be revisited when capacity allows.

---

## Content We Don't Create

**No paid advertising for Tahi.** Paid ads are a capability we offer clients. We do not run paid campaigns for ourselves currently. This is a budget constraint, not a permanent position.

**No cold email as a default.** Occasional highly personalised outreach in a Loom format is acceptable when the context genuinely warrants it. Mass cold email is not something we do.

**No external contributors.** All thought leadership on tahi.studio is authored by Liam or Staci. No guest posts inbound. We do not write guest posts for external sites either. Capacity is the constraint.

**No repurposing client work without permission.** We do not use client project details, screenshots, or outcomes in any content unless the client has explicitly approved it. Case studies require sign-off before publishing.

**No unattributed content.** If we share, quote, or reference someone else's idea or work, we credit them. We keep our thinking to our own unless we are explicitly crediting the source.

**No content that markets agency services to the freelancer audience.** The MailerLite list is predominantly freelancers and agency owners, not potential clients. Sending agency pitches to that segment undermines trust. See 13-marketing-strategy.md for the full audience segmentation logic.

---

## Notes

<!-- POSITIONAL CHANGE LOG -->
<!-- v1.0, March 2026: First version. Documents posting cadence and content types per channel. Refers to 02 for voice/format, 02b and 02c for personal LinkedIn, 13 for email strategy. Content We Don't Create section written fresh: covers paid ads, cold email, external contributors, client work, attribution, and the freelancer/client segment separation. Instagram noted as not currently active. -->

pages.title: Seo and Aeo Strategy
pages.content:
# 15: SEO and AEO Strategy
*Version 4.0, Last updated: March 2026*
*References: 04-ideal-client-profile.md, 17-website-architecture.md, 01-brand-dna.md, 07-services-and-pricing.md*

---

## Overview

This document is the source of truth for how Tahi Studio ranks in search and gets cited by AI engines. It covers keyword strategy, content direction, on-page fixes, competitive positioning, the AEO prompt matrix, and the roadmap to owning "webflow agency" globally.

Two things drive every decision here. First, the primary long-term ambition: rank number one for "webflow agency" — starting in New Zealand and Australia, expanding to the UK, then the US. Second, every SEO decision is made in service of a specific ICP: marketing directors and CMOs at enterprise-scale companies who feel the pain of a website that can't keep up with the business.

Everything else — domain authority plays, glossary terms that attract freelancers, the calculator — supports those two things without contradicting them.

---

## 1. Current State: What the Data Shows

### GSC Snapshot (90-Day Period to March 2026)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Total clicks | 382 |
| Total impressions | 26,639 |
| Average CTR | 1.43% |
| Average position | 12.6 |

The position trajectory is encouraging. December 2025 saw average positions in the 17–18 range. By March 2026 those same queries are sitting at 7–10. This is meaningful movement in a short window and reflects the early benefits of the content work done over the past few months.

The click volume is not yet where it needs to be. A 1.43% CTR against 26,639 impressions is low — there is a significant gap between visibility and click-through. That gap is the primary on-page opportunity.

### The Calculator: Domain Authority Asset, Not ICP Traffic

The Webflow Calculator drives approximately 59% of all organic clicks. This is a structural anomaly. The calculator attracts freelancers and agencies researching project pricing — not the enterprise marketing directors who are the actual ICP.

This is not a problem to solve quickly. It is a strategic reality to manage:

- The calculator builds domain authority and topical relevance for Webflow content broadly
- The freelancer and agency audience aligns with Nodeo's future user base — so the traffic has long-term commercial value beyond tahi.studio itself
- It should stay live and performant; it just should not be mistaken for ICP conversion traffic

The goal is to grow the enterprise-relevant side of organic traffic faster than the calculator grows, so the calculator's share of total clicks gradually dilutes toward a healthier proportion.

### Critical CTR Leaks

These are queries where Tahi has meaningful impressions but almost nobody clicks through. They represent the most immediate revenue-potential fix.

| Query | Position | Impressions | CTR | Issue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| webflow vs wordpress | 8.3 | 2,841 | 0.28% | Enormous opportunity, terrible CTR — meta title and description aren't compelling |
| FAQ page (aggregate) | ~18 | 828 | 0.12% | Meta title is "Frequently Asked Questions \| Tahi Studio" — no keyword, no hook |
| Webflow agency terms | ~12–15 | Multiple clusters | Low | Homepage missing "webflow agency" in title entirely |

### Homepage Meta Title: The Single Biggest Quick Fix

The homepage currently reads: **"Tahi Studio | Expert Webflow Design & Dev Subscriptions"**

This title contains no variation of "webflow agency." Every serious competitor has some version of "webflow agency" in their homepage title. This is the one change that will have the most immediate impact on organic impressions and click-through for the primary target keyword cluster.

Proposed fix: **"Tahi Studio | Webflow Agency for Enterprise Design & Development"**

This captures "webflow agency," signals enterprise positioning, and removes "subscriptions" — which can confuse enterprise buyers who don't immediately associate a subscription model with serious agency work. The pricing page handles subscriptions correctly. The homepage title doesn't need to carry that weight.

---

## 2. Strategic Intent: Ranking #1 for "Webflow Agency"

### The Ambition

The long-term goal is to be the first result when someone types "webflow agency" into Google — not just in New Zealand, but globally. That is a significant ambition given the current landscape, but it is achievable over a 3–5 year horizon with disciplined execution.

The path is sequential. Competing globally from day one is a waste of budget and effort. The roadmap:

**Phase 1 — 2026: Own NZ and AU.** "Webflow agency NZ" and "Webflow agency Australia" are winnable this year. Webflow penetration in these markets is growing, competition is thin, and Tahi has a natural home-ground advantage. These rankings build the domain authority and backlink profile needed to compete in larger markets.

**Phase 2 — 2027: Expand to UK.** The UK market has more Webflow competition, but Broworks is the main serious player and they are focused on enterprise HubSpot integrations, not pure Webflow agency positioning. There is a clear lane for Tahi here, particularly around the productised subscription model which UK marketing teams are starting to prefer over traditional retainer structures.

**Phase 3 — 2028 onwards: Compete in the US.** Flow Ninja owns a significant share of the enterprise Webflow conversation in the US. This is the hardest market. The strategy here is not to out-resource Flow Ninja — it is to out-niche them. Tahi's combination of Client First certification, sustainability commitment, and a productised model that enterprises can actually budget for will be differentiated by the time this phase begins in earnest.

### What It Actually Takes

Ranking for "webflow agency" globally requires three things working together:

**On-page authority on the homepage and key service pages.** The homepage title fix is step one. The homepage itself needs to position Tahi as an agency, not just a subscription service. Enterprise buyers search for agencies. They do not search for "webflow subscription service."

**Topical authority in the Webflow content cluster.** Google needs to understand that tahi.studio is the canonical source of Webflow knowledge, not just a company that mentions Webflow on its homepage. The blog, glossary, FAQ, and case studies all need to breathe Webflow — migrations, comparisons, use cases, the ecosystem, the limitations of competitors.

**External signals.** Backlinks from Webflow-adjacent domains (Finsweet, Made in Webflow, Webflow University, relevant SaaS review sites). The Webflow Premium Partner listing is an authority signal. The Client First certification page on finsweet.com (CF-00337) is a backlink from a domain with genuine authority in this space. Both should be working harder.

---

## 3. Competitive Analysis

### Flow Ninja

**Who they are:** The dominant enterprise Webflow agency globally. Based in Europe with a strong US presence. They built the WebOps category — the idea that enterprise web operations should be a continuous, managed function rather than a series of projects.

**Scale and positioning:** Reportedly $5,000+/month retainers. Portfolio includes Checkout.com, Upwork, Trustly, and similar enterprise names. 200+ enterprise clients.

**Their SEO play:** They are not trying to rank for "webflow agency" as a generic term — they own "webflow enterprise" and "webflow webops." That is a deliberate choice. Generic terms attract small businesses. Enterprise-qualified terms attract buyers with real budgets.

**What they are doing right:** Deep educational content around Webflow's enterprise capabilities. Case studies with household brand names. AEO is already in their positioning — they write about AI visibility directly.

**Where Tahi can beat them:** Flow Ninja is expensive and inaccessible for the mid-market enterprise buyer. A marketing director at a 200-person SaaS company cannot get a meeting with Flow Ninja without going through a lengthy qualification process. Tahi's productised model is faster to start, more predictable to budget, and more flexible to scale. The sustainability angle (carbon-negative, Trees That Count) is something Flow Ninja has no equivalent of. The Client First certification is a credibility signal that some enterprise buyers specifically look for and Flow Ninja does not lead with it.

**Keyword gaps to exploit:** Flow Ninja does not write much about WordPress migration (they are past that audience), Framer comparisons, or Lovable and vibe coding. These are growth-stage categories where Tahi can establish topical authority early.

### Flowout

**Who they are:** A subscription-based Webflow agency — the closest structural equivalent to Tahi's model. Strong Webflow SEO focus, multiple dedicated Webflow landing pages, heavy use of Webflow-specific long-tail terms.

**Their SEO play:** Flowout targets Webflow-specific long-tail searches. Content like "how to fix Webflow CMS limits" and "Webflow vs Framer for marketing teams" — middle-of-funnel content that catches buyers at the research stage before they are searching for an agency.

**What they are doing right:** Their content-to-conversion funnel is tight. They put SEO content in front of the research query, then convert it to a demo or trial. The subscription model means they are not afraid of lower-budget entry clients — they can qualify and upgrade.

**Where Tahi can beat them:** Flowout's content is competent but generic. It does not have a point of view. Tahi's brand voice — push back, everyone wins, sustainability as a built-in value — is distinctive in a category full of interchangeable agency blogs. Flowout also does not have meaningful NZ/AU presence. They are targeting US and European markets. The NZ/AU expansion is Tahi's lane right now.

**Keyword gaps to exploit:** Flowout does not write about WordPress migration from an enterprise perspective. They have no sustainability angle. Tahi's editorial position on "why we chose Webflow over WordPress for enterprise" is a differentiating content play Flowout cannot authentically replicate.

### Broworks

**Who they are:** UK and European enterprise agency with strong HubSpot integration focus. Webflow serves as the CMS and design layer. Enterprise-first, no SME or mid-market positioning.

**Their SEO play:** Broworks targets enterprise buyer search terms and leans heavily on case study authority. Their content tends toward "how enterprise marketing teams should think about their website" rather than platform-specific comparison content.

**What they are doing right:** Case study depth. They publish detailed project briefs that rank for company-specific searches and serve as trust signals in enterprise sales cycles.

**Where Tahi can beat them:** Broworks has no NZ/AU presence. Their HubSpot focus creates a blind spot — they are not writing about pure Webflow strategy. Their sustainability positioning is absent. They are also significantly more expensive and have a higher minimum engagement threshold than Tahi.

**Keyword gaps to exploit:** Broworks does not do Webflow vs competitor comparisons. Their audience is already enterprise so they are not writing migration content. Tahi can own the "enterprise considering moving to Webflow" content cluster without competing directly with Broworks' existing content.

### Positioning Matrix

| Dimension | Tahi | Flow Ninja | Flowout | Broworks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Model | Subscription + projects | Enterprise retainer | Subscription | Enterprise project + retainer |
| Price point | Mid-high | Premium ($5k+/mo) | Mid | Premium |
| Primary markets | NZ/AU + UK + US | US + Europe | US + Europe | UK + Europe |
| Sustainability angle | Yes (Trees That Count) | No | No | No |
| Client First certified | Yes (CF-00337) | Not prominent | No | No |
| Webflow Premium Partner | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| WordPress migration focus | Yes | No | Limited | No |
| Framer/Squarespace comparisons | Planned | No | Some | No |
| Enterprise Partner | Pursuing (2026 goal) | Yes | Partial | No |

---

## 4. Keyword Strategy

### How to Think About Keyword Tiers

Not all keywords are equal, and trying to rank for everything simultaneously is how agencies end up ranking for nothing important. The strategy is structured in three tiers based on intent, competition, and ICP alignment.

**Tier 1: Primary commercial intent.** Keywords typed by enterprise buyers who are ready to hire or seriously evaluating agencies. Converting even a small percentage of this traffic drives significant revenue. These take the longest to rank for and require the most authority.

**Tier 2: Research and comparison intent.** Keywords typed by people in evaluation mode — comparing platforms, understanding options, diagnosing problems. This content builds topical authority and captures buyers before they know who to hire.

**Tier 3: Topical authority and long-tail.** Terms that drive topical depth and domain authority signals. They attract the developer and freelancer audience whose traffic matters for authority-building even when they are not direct conversion targets.

### Tier 1: Primary Commercial Intent Keywords

| Keyword | Target Market | Priority |
|---|---|---|
| webflow agency | Global | Highest |
| webflow agency NZ | New Zealand | Highest |
| webflow agency australia | Australia | Highest |
| webflow agency uk | UK | High |
| webflow design agency | Global | High |
| webflow development agency | Global | High |
| webflow premium partner | Global | High |
| enterprise webflow agency | Global | Medium (2026–2027) |
| webflow website redesign | Global | Medium |
| webflow migration service | Global | Medium |

**Note on enterprise language:** Tahi is a Webflow Premium Partner (verified at webflow.com/@tahistudio) and Client First certified (CF-00337 at finsweet.com). The word "enterprise" can appear in content and keyword strategy to attract enterprise buyers — Tahi genuinely works with enterprise-scale clients. However, Tahi is not yet a Webflow Enterprise Partner. That is a specific Webflow tier with distinct programme requirements and Tahi is pursuing it as a 2026 goal. Content can describe the enterprise work Tahi does, but should not claim the Webflow Enterprise Partner designation until the programme formalises.

### Tier 2: Research and Comparison Intent Keywords

| Keyword | ICP Alignment | Content Status |
|---|---|---|
| webflow vs wordpress | High | Published — CTR leak, needs meta fix |
| wordpress to webflow migration | High | Not yet — priority content gap |
| framer vs webflow | High | Not yet — planned |
| squarespace to webflow | Medium | Not yet |
| webflow enterprise features | High | Not yet |
| webflow cms for enterprise | High | Not yet |
| why move from wordpress to webflow | High | Not yet — priority |
| webflow site speed | High | Not yet |
| webflow client first | High (quality signal) | Authority page exists |
| webflow pricing | Medium | Calculator serves this partially |
| lovable webflow integration | Medium | Not yet — first-mover opportunity |
| vibe coding website design | Medium | Not yet — first-mover opportunity |

### Tier 3: Topical Authority and Long-Tail

The calculator, glossary terms, and deep technical posts sit here. These drive domain authority and catch the developer and freelancer audience that builds backlinks and shares content. The value is indirect but real. See the content audit section for which existing content is working versus which is noise.

### The "Webflow Agency" Ranking Priority

The homepage, service pages, and case studies need to build authority for Tier 1. The blog and comparison content builds authority for Tier 2. Tier 3 happens as a byproduct of doing 1 and 2 well, plus the existing calculator and glossary content.

The single biggest lever for Tier 1 right now is the homepage meta title fix. After that, it is building case study depth and getting a credible backlink profile from Webflow-adjacent domains.

---

## 5. On-Page SEO Audit and Fixes

### Homepage (Critical)

**Current title:** "Tahi Studio | Expert Webflow Design & Dev Subscriptions"
**Problem:** No "webflow agency." No geographic modifier. "Subscriptions" can signal small-business service rather than enterprise agency.
**Proposed title:** "Tahi Studio | Webflow Agency for Enterprise Design & Development"
**Proposed description:** "Tahi Studio is a Webflow Premium Partner agency specialising in enterprise-scale design, development, and migrations. We push back, build properly, and make Webflow do more for your business."

### Calculator

**Current title:** "Free Webflow Calculator (formerly CashYourFlow) | Tahi Studio"
**Problem:** "(formerly CashYourFlow)" adds noise and dilutes brand clarity. A searcher seeing this in a SERP result gets confused about what the tool is.
**Proposed title:** "Free Webflow Project Cost Calculator | Tahi Studio"
**Proposed description:** "Estimate the real cost of your Webflow project. Based on $330k+ in verified quotes. Free to use, no signup required."

### FAQs Page

**Current title:** "Frequently Asked Questions | Tahi Studio"
**Problem:** Zero keyword value. No Webflow, no agency, no context for Google.
**Proposed title:** "Webflow Agency FAQs | Tahi Studio"
**Proposed description:** "Answers to the most common questions about working with Tahi Studio — our Webflow process, pricing model, timelines, and what enterprise clients should expect."

### Pricing Page

**Current title:** "Webflow Design & Development Pricing | Tahi Studio"
**Assessment:** Adequate. Could add "subscription" or "retainer" to capture those searchers.
**Proposed improvement:** "Webflow Design & Development Pricing and Plans | Tahi Studio"

### Glossary

**Current title:** "Webflow & Design Glossary | Tahi Studio"
**Assessment:** Adequate. The glossary is working as a topical authority play.
**No immediate change needed.**

### Blog

**Current title:** "Webflow Design & Development Blog | Tahi Studio"
**Assessment:** Adequate.
**No change needed.**

### NZ Landing Page

**Current title:** "Tahi Studio | Your Dedicated Webflow Partner in NZ"
**Assessment:** Good market specificity. Minor improvement available.
**Proposed improvement:** "Tahi Studio | Webflow Agency in New Zealand" — shorter, more direct, aligns with how someone actually searches.

### Free Site Audit

**Current title:** "Free Website Audit & SEO Report | Tahi Studio"
**Assessment:** Good. Descriptive and keyword-aligned.
**No change needed.**

### Webflow Pricing Report 2025

**Current title:** "Webflow Pricing Report 2025: Real Market Data | Tahi Studio"
**Assessment:** Excellent. The "$330k in verified project quotes" description is compelling and unusual. This should be the template for all data-led content — concrete figures, specific claims, defensible methodology.
**No change needed.**

### Partner Page

**Current title:** "Partner With Us | Tahi Studio Webflow Agency"
**Assessment:** Ironically the only page with "Webflow Agency" in the title. Good.
**No change needed.**

### Webflow Agency Landing Pages (to create in redesign)

Dedicated landing pages for key markets should be created as commercial pages — not blog posts. These serve the Tier 1 keyword cluster directly:

- `/webflow-agency-new-zealand` — target "webflow agency NZ" and "webflow agency new zealand"
- `/webflow-agency-australia` — target "webflow agency australia"
- `/webflow-agency-uk` — target "webflow agency uk"
- `/webflow-premium-partner` — consolidate authority around the partnership credential
- `/wordpress-to-webflow-migration` — commercial migration service page, not a blog post

---

## 6. Content Audit

### Blog: ICP Fit Categories

The blog is doing two jobs at once: attracting enterprise buyers and building topical authority with a developer and freelancer audience. Both are valid. The risk is that the content mix signals the wrong audience to Google's quality systems and that editorial capacity gets spread too thin.

**Category A: Strong ICP Fit — Keep, expand, update**

These posts directly address the pain points in the ICP document — attribution, performance, AI visibility, platform limitations, migration anxiety.

- Webflow vs WordPress comparison content
- "Why enterprise teams choose Webflow" angles
- Webflow performance and security topics
- Client First methodology posts
- Posts that demonstrate Tahi's strategic thinking, not just execution

New blog capacity should go here first.

**Category B: Domain Authority Play — Acceptable, stop expanding**

These posts attract the freelancer and agency audience. They build domain authority and topical depth. They are acceptable as long as they do not crowd out Category A and do not signal to enterprise buyers that Tahi is a small-business tool.

- Calculator-adjacent content ("how to price webflow projects")
- Freelancer workflow posts
- General Webflow tips and tricks
- Tool comparisons aimed at individual practitioners

Strategy: stop creating new Category B content. What exists is fine — let it accumulate authority quietly.

**Category C: Low-value — Review in redesign**

Posts with no SEO traction, no ICP alignment, and no authority signal. Low impressions, no meaningful ranking, no conversion path.

- Brand announcements without informational hooks
- Very short posts with no keyword depth
- Content that duplicates what the calculator or pricing page already says

Strategy: review these during the redesign. Expand what can be saved. Consolidate or remove the rest. Thin content at Tahi's current scale is more about diluting crawl budget than active penalisation — but the fix is editorial discipline going forward, not a mass deletion exercise.

### Priority Pending Posts

From the 26 pending posts in the architecture document, these have the most direct SEO and commercial value and should be prioritised accordingly.

**"The Top 7 Webflow Agencies of 2025 (An Honest Comparison)"**
This is the single highest-priority pending post in the entire backlog. Ranking for "best webflow agencies" and similar comparison terms captures enterprise buyers who are actively evaluating agencies — the most commercially valuable search moment. The format also provides natural backlink targets. The "honest" framing aligns with Tahi's brand voice. This post should go live before the redesign if possible. The earlier it is indexed and accumulating signals, the better.

**"Webflow Certified vs Premium Partner: Which is Right for You?"**
Builds direct authority around Tahi's actual credentials. Targets buyers who are beginning to understand the Webflow partner ecosystem. Positions Tahi's Premium Partner status as a meaningful qualifier, not just a badge.

**"The WordPress to Webflow Migration Guide for Marketing Teams"**
High ICP alignment. Many Tahi clients are still on WordPress — this is the trigger content that catches them at the "we know we need to move, we are not sure how" moment. Should be comprehensive enough to rank for "wordpress to webflow migration" as a long-form reference guide.

**"Why Your Site Isn't Showing Up in ChatGPT (And What To Do About It)"**
Direct AEO pain point from the ICP document. Marketing directors are watching their competitors appear in AI overviews and direct answers. Tahi has genuine expertise here. This post positions the studio as the authority on AEO for enterprise sites and naturally leads to a conversation about what Tahi can do to fix the problem.

**"Framer vs Webflow: What Enterprise Marketing Teams Actually Need"**
Framer is increasingly positioned as the design-led alternative to Webflow. This post captures buyers mid-evaluation before they have made a decision. The "for enterprise marketing teams" framing keeps it ICP-specific and differentiates it from the many generic Framer vs Webflow posts that focus on freelancers and solopreneurs.

**"Vibe Coding and Marketing: What Lovable Actually Does Well (and What It Doesn't)"**
First-mover opportunity. Lovable and similar AI-assisted design tools are getting significant attention in marketing circles. A post that honestly evaluates what vibe coding and AI design tools are good for — concept exploration, prototyping, communicating design intent — and where they fall short — production-quality enterprise sites, accessibility, performance, complex CMS — positions Tahi as a pragmatic expert rather than a threatened incumbent. The "here is how we use Lovable in our process" angle is both differentiating and genuinely useful.

### Glossary Strategy

The existing glossary has two layers worth distinguishing.

**Externally searchable terms:** Standard web design and Webflow terms that people actually search for. These build topical authority and drive impressions. Keep adding these with substance — not one-line definitions, but genuinely useful explainers that could be cited by AI tools answering definitional questions.

**Brand-specific terms:** "Greater Good Principle," "One Active Request," and similar Tahi-coined phrases. These are useful for internal clarity and for prospects who have already found Tahi. They do not drive external search volume. They are fine to have and good for the client relationship, but should not be counted toward topical authority building.

New glossary additions should default to externally searchable terms. Priority targets for new entries: Client First methodology, Webflow Localization, Webflow Enterprise tier, Webflow Optimize, Finsweet Attributes, Wized, headless Webflow, and migration-related terms.

### Content Angles Tahi Should Own

These are the distinctive editorial angles that align with Tahi's voice and have genuine first-mover potential. No major Webflow agency is currently writing about all of these with authority.

**WordPress limitations for enterprise teams.** The honest, unvarnished case for why enterprise marketing teams outgrow WordPress. Not a generic comparison — a post written for the marketing director sitting across the table from IT saying "can't we just fix the WordPress site?" This is empathy content for the person who already knows the answer and needs language to explain it to their organisation.

**Lovable and vibe coding as a concept design tool.** Tahi uses Lovable in the concepting stage. Document how. The angle is not "vibe coding replaces agencies" — it does not — but "here is how smart marketers use AI design tools to get from brief to aligned stakeholders faster, then hand off to a proper Webflow build." This is generous, honest, and positions Tahi as the sophisticated production partner rather than the threatened craftsperson.

**Framer and Squarespace as design-led dead ends for enterprise.** Both platforms have genuine strengths for specific use cases. Neither is suitable for enterprise-scale content operations, complex CMS requirements, or the kind of attribution and performance infrastructure enterprise marketing teams need. A post that acknowledges their genuine appeal while being clear about their limits is more credible than a hatchet job.

**Sustainable web design as a strategic choice.** Tahi plants native New Zealand trees through Trees That Count (1% of revenue) and is working toward carbon negativity. This is not greenwashing — it is a real operational commitment. The SEO angle: "sustainable web design agency" and related terms have near-zero competition. It is a genuine differentiator with a clear keyword lane.

**AI visibility for enterprise sites.** AEO is named in the ICP document as an explicit fear. Write the post that the ICP needs right now: what actually determines whether a brand appears in ChatGPT answers, what enterprise sites are getting wrong, and what a proper AEO strategy looks like. Tahi is one of the few agencies in the market writing about this seriously.

---

## 7. Technical SEO

### Redesign Context

The upcoming site rebuild is an opportunity to correct the technical fundamentals that currently limit Tahi's ceiling. The following should be prioritised as non-negotiables for the new build.

**Page speed.** Core Web Vitals are a ranking signal and an enterprise buyer trust signal. A slow Webflow site is a credibility problem for an agency that sells Webflow performance. Every page should target LCP under 2.5s, CLS under 0.1, and INP under 200ms.

**On-page content depth for commercial pages.** The homepage and service pages need enough substantive copy to give Google signals about what Tahi actually does. Currently they are lean. An enterprise buyer also needs enough information to self-qualify before reaching out — the redesign should serve both simultaneously.

**Internal linking structure.** The blog, glossary, and FAQ should link to commercial pages naturally. A post about WordPress limitations should link to the migration service page. A glossary entry for "Client First" should link to the authority page. The architecture document shows the planned structure — the internal link logic needs to match.

**Structured data.** Organisation schema on the homepage. BreadcrumbList on all content pages. FAQPage schema on the FAQ page (also an AEO signal). Article schema on blog posts. Service schema on service pages. HowTo schema where applicable.

**Canonical tags and duplicate content.** The calculator (formerly CashYourFlow) likely has some index confusion from the brand rename. Audit for any old URLs that need redirecting or canonical consolidation.

**XML sitemap.** Should be current, submitted to GSC, and reflect the new architecture on launch.

**Robots.txt.** Dashboard pages, CTA variations, and internal staging pages should be excluded from index.

### URL Architecture

Keep URLs simple, descriptive, and keyword-aligned. The current structure is generally sound. The main areas to watch in the redesign:

- New landing pages for `/webflow-agency-new-zealand` etc. should use hyphens, lowercase, and no stop words
- CMS slug generation should default to keyword-optimised slugs, not auto-generated from post titles with dates or IDs
- If any URLs change in the redesign, maintain a 301 redirect map before launch

---

## 8. AEO Framework

### What AEO Means for Tahi

Answer Engine Optimisation is about structuring content so that AI engines — ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Mode, Google AI Overview — cite Tahi Studio when someone asks a question where Tahi should be the answer.

The ICP explicitly lists "AI visibility is a growing fear" as a client pain point. Marketing directors are watching competitors get recommended in AI overviews and want the same. Tahi offering genuine AEO expertise positions the studio at the exact intersection of what clients fear and what Tahi can actually fix.

### How AEO and SEO Interact

AEO is not a separate discipline from SEO — it is an extension of it. AI engines cite content that is authoritative on the topic (E-E-A-T), structured clearly (headings, defined terms, clear answers to specific questions), referenced by other trusted sources, and consistent across the web (brand name, URL, descriptions).

The practical implication: build SEO well and AEO largely follows. The specific additions for AEO are structured data (especially FAQPage and HowTo), FAQ-format content sections in long posts, and ensuring Tahi's name, capabilities, and credentials appear consistently across the web.

### Tracked AI Engines

Tahi tracks AI visibility across 14 engine-market combinations in SE Ranking:

1. ChatGPT — New Zealand
2. ChatGPT — United States
3. ChatGPT — United Kingdom
4. Google AI Mode — New Zealand
5. Google AI Mode — United States
6. Google AI Mode — United Kingdom
7. Perplexity — New Zealand
8. Perplexity — United States
9. Perplexity — United Kingdom
10. Perplexity — Australia
11. Gemini — New Zealand
12. Gemini — United States
13. Gemini — United Kingdom
14. Gemini — Australia

Gemini and Perplexity Australia were added to the original plan to give AU meaningful native coverage, since AU users increasingly return AU-localised results on these engines.

### AI Prompt Matrix: Design Logic

Every tracked prompt passes a single test before it is included: *would a useful AI answer to this question realistically name or recommend a specific Webflow agency?* If the answer is an explanation, a definition, a how-to list, or general advice — with no natural reason to cite an agency — the prompt is cut.

This principle was refined through iteration. Earlier versions of this matrix included definitional queries ("What is Webflow Client First?", "What is sustainable web design?", "What is vibe coding?") and meta-AEO queries that generate informational responses, not agency recommendations. Those have been removed. The remaining prompts are ones where Tahi being named in the answer is the desired and realistic outcome.

The total budget is 150–200 tracked instances across all engines and markets combined. The calculation: unique prompts multiplied by the number of engine-market combinations each prompt is assigned to equals total tracked instances.

### AI Prompt Matrix (Live in SE Ranking as of March 2026)

**9 global prompts — tracked across all 14 engine-market combinations**

| Prompt | Rationale |
|---|---|
| What is the best Webflow agency? | Primary commercial intent — direct agency recommendation |
| Which Webflow agencies have Client First certification? | Credential query — Tahi is on the certified list |
| What are the costs of moving from WordPress to Webflow? | Cost questions naturally surface agency options |
| How do I migrate from WordPress to Webflow? | Process query — AI responses often close with agency referral |
| Which Webflow agencies offer subscription services? | Tahi's core model — highly specific agency list query |
| What is the best Webflow agency for enterprise companies? | Primary ICP intent — direct enterprise agency recommendation |
| What Webflow agencies specialise in B2B websites? | Niche qualification — Tahi's positioning fits precisely |
| Which Webflow agencies work with enterprise marketing teams? | ICP-specific — AI will list agencies that match this brief |
| What makes a good Webflow agency? | Evaluative query — AI responses cite examples of strong agencies |

*9 prompts × 14 combos = 126 tracked instances*

**3 NZ-specific prompts — tracked across 4 NZ engine-market combinations**
*(ChatGPT NZ, Google AI Mode NZ, Perplexity NZ, Gemini NZ)*

| Prompt |
|---|
| What is the best Webflow agency in New Zealand? |
| Who are the top web design agencies in New Zealand? |
| Which Webflow agencies operate in New Zealand? |

*3 prompts × 4 NZ combos = 12 tracked instances*

**Total: 12 unique prompts, 138 tracked instances**

### Content Required to Convert Prompts into Citations

A tracked prompt only generates a citation if tahi.studio has content that directly and authoritatively answers the underlying question. SE Ranking tells you whether you appeared — the content strategy determines whether you can.

Priority content mapped to the live prompt matrix:

- **Best Webflow agency (global + NZ)** → homepage needs explicit agency framing and credentials above the fold; the pending "Top 7 Webflow Agencies" post is the single highest-leverage content piece for this cluster
- **Costs of moving from WordPress to Webflow** → a dedicated cost/migration guide that gives concrete figures, not a generic blog post; the Webflow Pricing Report is adjacent but does not directly answer this query
- **How do I migrate from WordPress to Webflow** → the priority WordPress to Webflow migration guide
- **Subscription services / enterprise / B2B / marketing teams** → service pages and homepage need to explicitly name these use cases; case studies from enterprise and B2B clients are the citation fodder AI engines need
- **What makes a good Webflow agency** → a post or page that sets out Tahi's criteria for what good looks like — this is both a content piece and a positioning asset

---

## 9. Authority Building (E-E-A-T)

### Current Credentials

Google's E-E-A-T framework — Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, Trustworthiness — rewards content from verifiable experts with demonstrated real-world experience. Tahi's credentials are strong in this framework. The task is making them visible and well-linked.

**Webflow Premium Partner**
URL: webflow.com/@tahistudio
This is the primary external authority signal. It should be linked from the homepage, referenced on every relevant service page, cited in blog posts where the partnership is relevant, and the anchor text from the homepage should be "Webflow Premium Partner" to match the term people actually search for.

**Client First Certification (CF-00337)**
URL: finsweet.com/client-first/certification/certified-experts/cf-00337
This is a backlink from a high-authority Webflow-adjacent domain. It signals technical expertise to both humans and search engines. It should be featured prominently on the authority page already created for it, linked from any blog post or glossary entry that discusses Client First methodology, and described in the context of what it means for clients — clean, maintainable code; handoff-friendly; industry-standard framework.

**The Webflow Pricing Report**
The "$330k in verified project quotes" positioning is a first-party data claim — exactly the kind of Experience signal that E-E-A-T rewards. This report should be updated annually and cross-promoted aggressively. It is the kind of primary data that gets cited by other sources and earns backlinks organically.

**Lumos**
Tahi's Lumos profile (where active) is another external citation. Third-party directories and review platforms that list Tahi as a verified Webflow agency are all E-E-A-T signals.

### Author Pages and Team Credibility

Blog posts from named authors with visible expertise carry more E-E-A-T weight than posts with generic authorship. The team section is marked "Not Ready" in the architecture document. When it launches, it should include genuine professional bios that reference years of experience, Webflow-specific credentials, past work and named clients where permissible, and links to profiles on Webflow.com and LinkedIn. Even a single well-constructed author bio for the primary content author would improve the authority signal on every blog post.

### Third-Party Citations

AI engines and search algorithms weight content that appears across multiple credible sources. Tahi should be listed on Made in Webflow (if not already), active in Webflow community forums and Discord with links back to tahi.studio, quoted in or contributing to third-party content about Webflow, and cited by the brands and tools in the affiliate and partner stack: Outseta, Optibase, Finsweet, ManyRequests.

---

## 10. Reporting and Measurement

### Primary Tools

**Google Search Console (sc-domain:tahi.studio)**
Source of truth for actual search performance. Used to track total clicks, impressions, CTR by page and by query, average position trends, and index coverage issues. Check monthly at minimum; check weekly during and after major content or technical changes.

**SE Ranking**
Primary tool for keyword rank tracking and AI prompt monitoring. Four markets configured: NZ, UK, US, AU. The AI Result Tracker module monitors 14 engine-market combinations for the prompt matrix: ChatGPT (NZ, US, UK), Google AI Mode (NZ, US, UK), Perplexity (NZ, US, UK, AU), and Gemini (NZ, US, UK, AU). Cost-sensitive — run targeted queries rather than broad exploration.

### Key Metrics to Track Monthly

| Metric | Current | 6-Month Target | 12-Month Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| Organic clicks (90-day) | 382 | 700+ | 1,500+ |
| Average CTR | 1.43% | 2.5%+ | 3%+ |
| Average position | 12.6 | 8.0 | 5.0 |
| "Webflow agency NZ" position | Not ranking | Top 10 | Top 3 |
| AI prompt citations | Baseline TBD | 20% of prompts cited | 40% of prompts cited |
| Calculator share of total clicks | ~59% | ~45% | ~35% |

### Reporting Cadence

**Monthly:** GSC snapshot, SE Ranking position report, AI prompt citation count. A 30-minute exercise that produces the most important operational view of how the strategy is working.

**Quarterly:** Full audit of which content is driving commercial enquiries versus domain authority only. Review the CTR leak list for new entries. Assess whether the AI prompt matrix needs new prompts added or under-performing ones removed.

**Annually:** Update the Webflow Pricing Report with fresh data. Review the competitive landscape for any new entrants or significant shifts in how Flow Ninja, Flowout, or Broworks are positioning. Reassess the market expansion phase — if NZ/AU targets are achieved, begin formal UK campaign.

---

## 11. Quick Reference: Priority Actions

In rough order of expected impact, these are the actions to take before and during the redesign.

1. **Fix the homepage meta title.** Change from "Expert Webflow Design & Dev Subscriptions" to "Webflow Agency for Enterprise Design & Development." Five minutes of work, immediate ranking signal improvement.

2. **Publish "The Top 7 Webflow Agencies."** Highest-priority pending post. Get it indexed and accumulating signals as early as possible.

3. **Fix the calculator meta title.** Remove "(formerly CashYourFlow)."

4. **Fix the FAQ page meta title.** "Webflow Agency FAQs | Tahi Studio."

5. **Publish the WordPress to Webflow migration guide.** Long-form, ICP-focused, comprehensive enough to rank for the head term.

6. **Add structured data.** FAQPage schema on the FAQ page at minimum. Organisation schema on homepage.

7. **Publish the Framer vs Webflow and Lovable/vibe coding posts.** First-mover advantage while competitors aren't writing about these seriously.

8. **Expand sustainability content.** A dedicated sustainability page turns the Trees That Count commitment into a rankable asset.

9. **Set up the AI prompt matrix in SE Ranking.** Begin tracking baseline citation rates now, before the redesign, so there is a pre/post comparison.

10. **Create the market-specific Webflow agency landing pages** (NZ, AU, UK) as part of the redesign.

---

## Positional Change Log

*For internal reference. Tracks where the strategy has evolved and why.*

| Topic | v2.0 Position | v3.0 Position | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| AI prompt count | 150 base prompts implied per engine | 27 unique prompts, ~176 total across all engines | Corrected to reflect actual tracking budget (150–200 total, not per engine) |
| Homepage meta title | Identified as gap | Specific replacement title drafted | Confirmed via Webflow MCP — no "webflow agency" present in current title |
| Enterprise agency language | Unclarified | Premium Partner confirmed; Enterprise Partner is 2026 goal | Prevents misrepresentation of current credential tier |
| Competitor analysis | Broworks only | Flow Ninja, Flowout, Broworks with strategic depth | Expanded to include the two most directly comparable competitors |
| Content audit | Not present | ICP-fit categories A, B, and C established | Provides framework for editorial decisions going forward |
| Glossary strategy | Not addressed | External vs brand-specific terms distinguished | Focuses new glossary additions on searchable terms |
| Market expansion | Not sequenced | NZ/AU → UK → US roadmap established | Reflects realistic path to global "webflow agency" ranking |
| Calculator traffic | Noted as traffic source | Acknowledged as ICP mismatch, retained as domain authority and Nodeo audience play | More honest about what the calculator traffic is and is not |
| Content angles | Not included | WordPress limitations, Lovable/vibe coding, Framer/Squarespace limitations named | Reflects Liam's editorial voice and first-mover opportunities |
| Sustainability angle | Mentioned in passing | Treated as a distinct keyword cluster with near-zero competition | Elevated from background fact to active SEO strategy |
| AI prompt count (v3.0 → v4.0) | 27 unique prompts, ~176 instances | 12 unique prompts, 138 instances | Refined prompt selection — cut all definitional and informational queries; kept only prompts where an AI answer would realistically name a specific agency |
| AI prompt philosophy (v3.0 → v4.0) | Broad topical coverage including definitional, sustainability, vibe coding, and meta-AEO queries | Agency-recommendation queries only | Test: "would a useful AI answer to this prompt cite a specific Webflow agency?" If no, the prompt is out |
| Engine-market combos (v3.0 → v4.0) | 9 combos (ChatGPT, Google AI Mode, Perplexity × NZ/US/UK) | 14 combos — added Gemini (NZ, US, UK, AU) and Perplexity AU | Gemini and Perplexity AU added for native AU coverage; Gemini growing share of AI answer traffic |
| Prompt matrix status (v3.0 → v4.0) | Theoretical / planned | Live in SE Ranking as of March 2026 | Matrix entered and tracking active |

pages.content:
# Website Architecture Fact-Check Report
*Prepared: 2026-03-16*
*Source: Webflow MCP API vs. `17-website-architecture.md` (Version 2.0)*

---

## Methodology

Every page, CMS collection, and CMS item was pulled directly from the Webflow API and compared against the architecture file. No changes were made. This is analysis only.

---

## 1. Site-Level Details

| Field | Architecture File | Webflow (Live) | Status |
|-------|------------------|----------------|--------|
| Site name | Tahi Studio | Tahi Studio | ✅ Match |
| Base URL | https://www.tahi.studio | www.tahi.studio + tahi.studio (both active) | ✅ Match |
| Version last updated | `[date]` — placeholder, never filled in | — | ❌ Missing |
| Site created | Not stated | 2025-04-30 | — |
| Site last updated (Webflow) | Not stated | 2026-03-15 | — |
| Site last published | Not stated | 2026-02-18 (subdomain); 2026-02-16 (custom domains) | ⚠️ Note: site has been edited since last publish |

> **Note:** The site was last edited in Webflow on **2026-03-15** but last published on **2026-02-18** (to the Webflow subdomain) and **2026-02-16** (to the custom domains www.tahi.studio and tahi.studio). There are unpublished changes sitting in Webflow.

---

## 2. Static Pages

Webflow has **26 pages total**. The architecture file accounts for some of them across different sections (Static, CTA, CMS Templates). Below is a full reconciliation.

### 2a. Pages in Architecture — Verified Against Webflow

| # | Page | Arch Slug | Arch Status | Webflow Draft? | Webflow Status | Match? |
|---|------|-----------|-------------|----------------|----------------|--------|
| 1 | Home | `/` | [Linkable] | No | Live | ✅ |
| 2 | Pricing | `/pricing` | [Linkable] | No | Live | ✅ |
| 3 | NZ Landing Page | `/nz` | [Not Ready] | No | **Live (not draft)** | ❌ Mismatch — marked Not Ready in arch but live in Webflow |
| 4 | Blog | `/blog` | [Linkable] | No | Live | ✅ |
| 5 | Glossary | `/resources/glossary` | [Linkable] | No | Live | ✅ |
| 6 | 404 | `/404` | [Not Ready] | No | Live (system page) | ✅ |
| 7 | Password | `/401` | [Not Ready] | No | Live (system page) | ✅ |
| 8 | Contact | `/contact` | [Linkable] (CTA section) | No | Live | ✅ |
| 9 | Free Site Audit | `/free-site-audit` | [Linkable] (CTA section) | No | Live | ✅ |
| 10 | Webflow Project Calculator | `/webflow-project-calculator` | [Linkable] (CTA section) | No | Live | ✅ |
| 11 | Webflow Pricing Report 2025 | `/resources/webflow-pricing-report-2025` | [Linkable] (CTA section) | No | Live | ✅ |

### 2b. Pages in Webflow — NOT in Architecture File

These pages exist in Webflow but are completely absent from the architecture file:

| Page | Slug | Draft? | Created | Last Updated | Notes |
|------|------|--------|---------|-------------|-------|
| **Affiliates** | `/partner-with-us` | **No (Live)** | 2026-02-16 | 2026-02-17 | ❌ Fully live page, not documented anywhere in architecture |
| Services (static) | `/services` | Yes (Draft) | 2025-08-19 | 2025-11-10 | Probably intentional placeholder |
| Styleguide | `/utils/styleguide` | Yes (Draft) | 2023-04-25 | 2026-02-19 | Internal use, probably intentional omission |
| Structure | `/static-template-slug-1750337659829` | Yes (Draft) | 2025-06-19 | 2025-08-18 | Internal placeholder, probably intentional |

> **Issue:** The **Affiliates page** (`/partner-with-us`) is fully live, has SEO metadata, and was created in February 2026. It is entirely absent from the architecture file. The affiliate signup link listed in the architecture's Social/Promo section (`https://tahi-studio.getrewardful.com/signup`) is an external link — it does not replace the on-site `/partner-with-us` page.

---

## 3. CMS Template Pages

Architecture lists 7 CMS template pages. All 7 exist in Webflow. No slug errors. However, two additional template pages exist in Webflow that are not in the architecture:

| Template | Arch Status | Webflow Draft? | Notes |
|----------|-------------|----------------|-------|
| Scripts Template `/resources/scripts` | [Not Ready] | No (not draft) | ⚠️ Template is live in Webflow, but both CMS items are drafts. Architecture intent correct but technically inaccurate. |
| Glossaries Template `/resources/glossary` | [Linkable] | No | ✅ |
| Blog Posts Template `/blog` | [Linkable] | No | ✅ |
| Team Members Template `/company/team` | [Not Ready] | No | ✅ |
| Legal Documents Template `/legal` | [Linkable] | No | ✅ |
| Categories Template `/resources/categories` | [Not Ready] | No | ✅ |
| FAQs Template `/resources/faqs` | [Linkable] | No | ✅ |
| **Testimonials Template `/testimonials`** | **Not in arch** | No | ❌ Missing from architecture |
| **Services Template `/services`** | **Not in arch** | No | ❌ Missing from architecture |

### Architecture Summary Table Error — CMS Templates
The architecture summary says CMS Template Pages: **3 linkable, 4 not ready**.
Counting from the architecture's own table: **4 linkable** (Glossaries, Blog Posts, Legal, FAQs) and **3 not ready** (Scripts, Team Members, Categories).
The summary is inverted — the numbers are swapped.

---

## 4. CMS Collections

### 4a. Collections in Architecture vs. Webflow

| Collection | In Architecture | In Webflow | Notes |
|------------|----------------|------------|-------|
| FAQs | ✅ | ✅ | Count discrepancy — see below |
| Categories | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ Match |
| Legal Documents | ✅ | ✅ | Date discrepancies — see below |
| Team Members | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ Match |
| Blog Posts | ✅ | ✅ | Count and title discrepancies — see below |
| Glossaries | ✅ | ✅ | Count discrepancy + slug/date errors — see below |
| Scripts | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ Match |
| Case Studies | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ Match |
| **Testimonials** | ❌ Not in arch | ✅ | Missing from architecture |
| **Services** | ❌ Not in arch | ✅ | Missing from architecture |

---

## 5. FAQs — Detailed Audit

**Architecture says:** 43 items [Not Ready]
**Webflow actual:** 51 items — all published, none archived, none draft

### 5a. Count Discrepancy: 8 Missing FAQs

The following 8 FAQs exist in Webflow (all published **2025-12-14**) and are **not listed** in the architecture file. They are the Webflow Pricing Report FAQs:

| Question | Slug | Published |
|----------|------|-----------|
| How much should I budget for a Webflow website in 2026? | how-much-should-i-budget-for-a-webflow-website-in-2026 | 2025-12-14 |
| What is the standard hourly rate for a Webflow developer right now? | what-is-the-standard-hourly-rate-for-a-webflow-developer-right-now | 2025-12-14 |
| Does it cost more to hire a designer who also develops? | does-it-cost-more-to-hire-a-designer-who-also-develops | 2025-12-14 |
| Is it cheaper to hire a Junior developer for my project? | is-it-cheaper-to-hire-a-junior-developer-for-my-project | 2025-12-14 |
| Which industries are investing the most in Webflow? | which-industries-are-investing-the-most-in-webflow | 2025-12-14 |
| Does the location of the agency affect the price? | does-the-location-of-the-agency-affect-the-price | 2025-12-14 |
| What is the "Efficiency Bonus" mentioned in the report? | what-is-the-efficiency-bonus-mentioned-in-the-report | 2025-12-14 |
| Why is there such a big gap in project quotes (e.g. $1k vs $10k)? | why-is-there-such-a-big-gap-in-project-quotes-e-g-1k-vs-10k | 2025-12-14 |

### 5b. Potential Duplicate FAQ

Two FAQs exist that are very similar in scope:
- "Who actually works on my projects?" (slug: `who-actually-works-on-my-projects`)
- "Who will actually be working on my projects?" (slug: `who-will-be-working-on-my-projects`)

Both are published. This may be intentional but is worth reviewing.

### 5c. All 43 Architecture-Listed FAQs — Verified ✅
All 43 FAQs listed in the architecture file were confirmed present and published in Webflow. Slugs match.

---

## 6. Categories — Detailed Audit

**Architecture says:** 13 items [Linkable]
**Webflow actual:** 13 items — all published

✅ **All 13 categories match exactly** — names, slugs, and CMS IDs all verified correct.

---

## 7. Legal Documents — Detailed Audit

**Architecture says:** 2 items [Linkable] — published 26/08/25, updated 22/08/25 (both)
**Webflow actual:** 2 items — both published, not archived, not draft

| Document | Arch Published | Arch Updated | Webflow lastPublished | Webflow fieldData updated-date | Status |
|----------|---------------|-------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|--------|
| Privacy Policy | 26/08/25 | 22/08/25 | **2025-10-01** | 2025-08-22 | ❌ Published date wrong (should be 01/10/25); updated date ✅ |
| Terms of Service | 26/08/25 | 22/08/25 | **2025-10-26** | **2025-10-26** | ❌ Both dates wrong (published: 26/10/25, updated: 26/10/25) |

Both documents have been re-published since the architecture file was written. The Terms of Service was also substantively updated on 2025-10-26 — the architecture file does not reflect this.

---

## 8. Team Members — Detailed Audit

20. **Blog post main section count wrong** — header says "25 published" but the table has 28 rows.

### Minor
